output: Mr President, first of all, I would like to congratulate Mr Sieber on his work, because his report includes many of the concerns expressed in this
in this House on these crucial issues for the whole of the European Union: water scarcity and drought, matters that are no longer just a problem for southern European countries.

I am pleased that this project incorporates some of the reflections made in the opinion report that I drafted in the Committee on Agriculture and Rural Development, in favour of agricultural activity, highlighting, for example, the role played by farmers in the sustainable management of available resources.

It also refers to drought and water scarcity as factors that increase the prices of raw materials, something that I think is very important to highlight in the current climate, so that we bear in mind not only the environmental dimension of this problem, but also some of its most significant economic consequences.

The Committee on the Environment included the idea of creating a European Drought Observatory, an action also mentioned in the report of the Committee on Agriculture, and which I hope will one day be reflected in concrete actions.

The text to be put to the vote tomorrow, however, includes a proposal made by the Committee on Agriculture, calling for the launching of an economic adaptation fund against drought, a fund that would benefit all economic sectors, including agriculture.

As for me, I would like to make it clear that I will continue to defend the establishment of this fund, an idea that I will propose to Parliament again when the Commission's communication on adaptation to climate change is published in the coming months.

It seems to me that, after the Solidarity Fund was established, which was created to alleviate the damage caused by climatic disasters, it is time to consider an instrument that acts ex ante and is intended to finance preventive measures to reduce the environmental and economic cost of these climatic disasters.
Atletico Madrid, the fans and even the Spanish police are being mistreated by UEFA.
However, the case goes beyond the particular, because those federative bodies increase the penalties for those who turn to ordinary courts.
This medieval concept, this law of the funnel, is incompatible with the law, with European institutions, from which we must react.
And we will do it, because those medieval lords, arbitrary wielders of the gallows and the knife, must get in line with respect for the law and the ordinary procedural guarantees of our Europe.
Mr President, Commissioner, terrorism is a global phenomenon, as is serious organised crime, and so the measures must be proportionate and effective.

I have taken good note of the answers you have given to the questions that were appropriate. It is true that we must demand guarantees, it is true that this is a sensitive issue, but it is no less true that it is absolutely inexcusable not to come up with a globalised and harmonised response.
For some, terrorism is a little far away, and they are more concerned about individual liberties.
I am concerned about both the individual and collective ones, and it is absolutely necessary that we start wherever we can.
If we are going to start with air transport, where the companies already have that data, let us start there.

Let us demand guarantees, let us see what the scope of application is, let us start with international transport relations and, be careful, we will have to follow up with internal ones, because terrorists often do not come from outside, but from within.
Ask the United States and ask others, because that is the way it is and that is the way we will have to approach it.
Mr President, I would like to thank the Commission for having tabled this proposal.
I think it is a positive proposal and one that will help us to reduce unnecessary administrative burdens.
Now, as the speakers who have preceded me have said, Parliament has the obligation to bring down to earth what are very generous and very altruistic proposals on the part of the Commission.

And the reality is that there is a national reality in which there are 27 countries with different legal systems and with very different languages, which requires that in each of those countries we adapt to the realities of the country, including the issue of languages.

Specifically, as Mr Gauzès has just said, and as previous speakers have said, it is important that the recognition of these national specialisms should not lead to increased costs, that the charges should be uniform and that governments should eventually assume that additional cost.
But there is also another issue, and that is the issue of translations.
Specifically, in the European Union we do not have a common language.
In the 27 countries, we usually speak very different languages from each other, and even in some countries, as the Commissioner said, different languages are spoken, and we live with this.
And from a legal point of view, we also find that the legal documents are different.
In the Legal Commission, the Legal Commission has proposed, for example, an amendment on the authentication of minutes of documents, together with the authentication of the translation.
At the moment, the Committee on Legal Affairs is preparing a report on the recognition of notarial acts in different countries of the European Union, which should complement this report.
In short, I think the Commission has done well to present this report.
The rapporteur has done a magnificent job and I believe we have all strived to produce a legislative text proposal that can be recognised throughout the Union, guaranteeing the
and based on the reality of an Union that is being built, which has different national legal systems and different levels of access to electronic communication.

Mr President, a large majority of farmers and livestock breeders are awaiting decisions and prior agreements that the Agriculture Council of Ministers of the Union will adopt between today and tomorrow on this check.

the CAP, because this is not just a simple review, but will bring about profound changes, such as the disappearance of many farms, if this cutback on direct aid proposed by the European Commission goes ahead.

Modulation is the central point of this parliamentary report and could be one of the few elements of Parliament ’ s opinion to have some impact on the negotiations being held by the Union ’ s agriculture ministers.
We have been opposing for years any increase in what we euphemistically call modulation, because if the rural development policy does not have sufficient funds, it is not
not because of a lack of resources within the CAP spending limit, but because of a complete lack of political will to give the Community budget that necessary second pillar.
Do they want to liberalise markets?
Of course, do it, but with the same requirements and conditions for all producers, both inside and outside the Union.

At the moment, the European Union is gambling on the future of many farmers and stockbreeders, and I would particularly like to refer to fragile sectors such as sheep, cattle and tobacco, whose survival in many producing areas will depend on this check.

Some producers, such as those in the wine sector, are no longer asking for help, but simply want to be allowed to leave the market with dignity, because they have done the sums and the only way to survive is to reduce the volumes produced.

Therefore, the only option left for many producers is to abandon production, and their space will undoubtedly be taken up by imports from third countries, because consumers will not stop consuming and the world will not stop increasing the number of consumers at a staggering rate.
Parliament should issue a prudent opinion and the Council should listen to it.

I would like to thank the Chair of the Committee on Agriculture and Rural Development, Mr Parish, for the work he has done to ensure that Parliament is heard and is not so clearly and ostentatiously ignored by the Council and the Commission as it has been so far.
Mr President, first of all, I would like to congratulate the rapporteur, Mr Capoulas Santos, on his excellent work.
I think you have found balance and moderation in your report.
I think that what is important at this stage in the debate is to send a message of stability to our farmers.
European farmers need stability and solutions more than ever.
Sufficient transitional periods are needed, as well as maintaining specific measures that are necessary for sectors in difficulty.
Commissioner, the same solutions are not valid for all sectors.
We must remember that there are sectors that are going through real difficulties.
The common agricultural policy should not be the problem, but the solution, and for that we need a strong first pillar.
We believe in and support rural development, but we do not believe that the modulation proposed by the Commission is the solution.
It is not a question of strengthening rural development to the detriment of the first pillar.
We agree that the single payment scheme needs to be simplified and that market instruments need to be reviewed.
But that does not mean dismantling them.
If we do it well, we will be laying the foundations for a common agricultural policy beyond 2013; if not, we will be putting an end to the activity of many European farmers.
We are dealing with a plan that the citrus sector has been waiting for a long time, not only because of the difficulties it is experiencing, but also because of the worrying fall in consumption.
we are dealing with a public health promotion plan. I believe it should be a European plan, and I also support full EU funding.

I do not agree that parents should be allowed to contribute to the co-financing, because the same old thing will happen: children whose parents can afford to pay will eat fruit and vegetables at school, and those whose parents cannot will be left out of the programme.
I think it has to be a plan financed by the Community so that its distribution is even.
For this reason, I would also like to stress that we need to focus on educational measures.

It is not just about a student eating an apple, but about knowing what type of apple they are eating, knowing its nutritional properties and understanding the beneficial effects that this fruit has on their health and development.
Of course, these must be products of the highest quality, and seasonal fruit should be given priority. When possible, I support the Community origin of our fruit and vegetables.

I believe that we will immediately notice the beneficial effects of this plan, which I applaud and for which I want to congratulate the Commissioner, of course, for the present and future health of our children and their education.

food, but I also believe it is very important for the horticultural sector, a productive sector which constitutes a genuine European cultural heritage, because thanks to it we can enjoy a balanced, healthy and varied diet.
I think it is worth investing in this plan to distribute fruit and vegetables in our schools.
I think the budget is a little short because, once again, I am in favour of full EU funding for such an ambitious plan.
Mr President, I would ask you not to include these first few words, please, in the timekeeping machine, because my
My first words are to add my condolences to the message expressed by this House to Mrs Klamt and, above all, to congratulate the rapporteurs.
Now I really start.

Mr President, I would like to congratulate the rapporteurs because with these two proposals we are taking a qualitative step towards a common immigration policy and we are promoting legal immigration, a key pillar of the global approach we defend.
The general rights framework and the single residence and work permit for legal immigrants should be extended to the greatest number of workers.
For that reason, the Socialists are asking that no category of worker be excluded.
The so-called Blue Card offers an opportunity for immigrants to settle with their families and work in our countries.
It opens a single door to 27 labour markets.
However, this door should not only be open to highly qualified workers.
Therefore, Commissioner, we hope that the Commission will present its proposals on the other worker categories to us shortly.
Ladies and gentlemen, we must fight brain drain.
This blue card must not become a passport that encourages a drain of vital human resources in developing countries.
For this reason, the Socialist Group wants to ensure that for every qualified professional coming to Europe, we pay for the training of a new professional in the country of origin.
Finally, Europe must be an attractive destination not only for talents coming from outside the Union, but also for our own.
In 2007, almost 300 000 highly qualified Europeans went to work outside the Union.
Let us do everything we can to keep them in the European home.
Madam President, do you think that the closeness or otherwise of the candidates to the electors has anything to do with the level of participation in the European elections?
Do you agree that a country of 45 million inhabitants should have only one electoral constituency for elections to the European Parliament?
What can the Commission do to ensure that the Member States with the largest populations can have constituencies closer to their citizens?
Excuse me, but I have not heard the Vice-President answer my questions.
Madam President, you have given me a lot of information on the subject I raised, and I thank you for that detailed information.
I think the new stage of the Commission is important, and above all, the Bamako project of a European Union centre on immigration.

I know it has just opened, and it is probably very soon, but the question I am asking the Commission is: if the Bamako-Bali immigration project were to yield good results, does the Commission think it would be possible to extend that experience to the rest of the countries on the southern shore of the Mediterranean?

I thank you for your answer on behalf of Mr Yáñez-Barnuevo, but I would like to say to you, Commissioner, and to Mr Michel, that your actions, your words and your gestures are very necessary, in direct contact and
really with the Cuban democratic opposition in order to implement the Council ’ s priority conclusions and reduce the effects of the terrible Castro dictatorship in favour of freedom.
Mr President, on behalf of the Socialist Group, I want to express my group ’ s strongest condemnation of the assassinations being committed in this region of Somalia in the name of God.
Few atrocities have been committed by someone who has not invoked the name of God or of a religion.

Specifically, the way in which this atrocious murder was committed, we already know, a 13-year-old girl who is raped and then accused of adultery and that five men, if they can be called men, rush to stone her and even prevent anyone from trying to save her, in a stadium occupied by a thousand people.

This, together with piracy attacks, which this year off the coast of Somalia have reached the level of almost one hundred ships seized, constitutes a humanitarian situation that is absolutely unacceptable.
The international community cannot remain impassive to this.
We cannot remain impassive in the face of cowardice, in the face of religious appeals to commit barbarities.
Therefore, we have to re-establish order by supporting the legitimate Government of Somalia in regaining control of the whole country and establishing a state of law that respects human rights.
I believe that on few occasions have we been presented with such a clear situation and one that imposes obligations on us.
I don't think we can remain impassive here in this community of 500 million people and 27 countries, the most important in the world, without intervening in that situation.
I do not know how it can be done, but I am sure that it has to be done and that we have to intervene quickly.
The Socialist Group does not agree with the amendments that have been tabled at the last minute and that were not negotiated at the time.

We support the text of the joint resolution and we hope that this is the beginning of greater concern from the European Union about these types of humanitarian issues and about rejecting the abusive use of religious concepts to commit atrocities in the name of God.
I believe that the rapporteur and the speakers who have preceded me have highlighted the characteristics of this proposal for a directive from Parliament and the Council.
It is a recognition that armaments and ammunition are also part of the internal market, although, of course, with a series of limitations.
These are not normal goods, they are not sweets or entertainment items, but objects that require precise control.
Of course, the Treaty itself has limitations, Articles 30 and 296, which give Member States important responsibilities in this area.
The fact that we have a common market does not prevent the Member States from being obliged to comply with safety standards and from having the right to apply them when their own security is at risk.

The work done in the Committee on Legal Affairs and in the committees of opinion that have collaborated with them, in agreement with the Council, under the expert guidance of the rapporteur, Mrs Rühle, has been very positive.
I think that the text we are presenting today is a fairly balanced one.

Basically, although on paper it seems that there are many amendments, today we are limiting ourselves to just one amendment, which is amendment 63, which is the one that incorporates the spirit of the compromise. In that amendment, you can see a well-structured, coherent text that will allow the whole of this market to function.
Mr Toubon has stressed that this is related to other international texts.

For example, the adoption of the Oslo Convention in this same month, the signing of the Oslo Convention on the prohibition of cluster munitions, also the Convention on the limitations of anti-personnel mines and a whole series of international agreements and even European Union regulations aimed at limiting the use of weapons.

I believe that our model is not the model of free circulation of arms at any time, but rather of a regulated market, controlled by the Member States and also controlled from now on by the European Union institutions themselves.
Madam President, Commissioner, this proposal came from the Commission more than a year ago, with some really very ambitious objectives.
In the meantime, there have been financial upheavals in the world that seemed as if they were going to sink these measures, but we have finally reached a balanced agreement that does not
It does not satisfy almost anyone one hundred per cent, but it is a good agreement that maintains the final objectives, but makes it more affordable for companies to achieve them.

For that reason, we must especially thank and congratulate the rapporteur, Mrs Doyle, the rapporteurs for the other political groups and the French Presidency in particular, because their work has led to a good result for everyone.

I trust that in the near future circumstances will allow us to be much more ambitious and that other countries will also join this crusade to fight climate change that we in Europe so much want to carry out.

Mr President, Commissioner, this was a good proposal as it came from the Commission, not only because of the reductions in sulphur, but also because of its new Article 7a, which required suppliers to provide
gradual reduction in greenhouse gas emissions and because it solved the old problem of vapour pressure for petrol-alcohol blends in countries such as mine with hot summers.
Precisely this did not pass the vote in the Committee on the Environment, and this worried many of us.

Today, we have an agreement on the table that softens the application of Article 7 bis and brings back the waiver on vapour pressure, while, of course, making the conditions for accessing the exception more stringent.
And, as with the rest of this climate package, the agreement does not satisfy everyone one hundred percent, but it is acceptable to everyone.

I would like to thank Mrs Corbey for her excellent work, for the tenacity she has shown in solving problems, Mr Turmes and, of course, the French Presidency, who have also shown great effectiveness here.
As the report says, according to the 2007 estimates, Zimbabwe has a population of 12 million, almost half of which is starving.

Life expectancy is 36 years. And only 40% of the teachers in the country are working, and only a third of the students attend class. So Mugabe is not only killing the present, he is killing the future.
Human rights repression and mutilation are also on the agenda.
Human rights defenders in Zimbabwe have been systematically targeted for arbitrary detention, arrest and torture.
The legislation introduced by the Government severely restricts freedom of expression, assembly, movement and association.
I would like to mention some of them, as the Government has introduced increasingly repressive rules, which have been used against these human rights defenders and activists.

For example, the Access to Information and Privacy Protection Act, used by the Government to effectively silence journalists, the Public Order and Security Act, which has severely limited the freedom of assembly and hundreds of

human rights defenders, including staff from independent media, have been arbitrarily detained, and also the Law on Private Voluntary Organisations, re-established in 2002 by the Government and allegedly used to intimidate and harass NGOs.
Madam President, in my mother tongue, Basque, which is not official in this House, ' Mugabe ' means ' without limits ', a good nickname for this character.
The dictator of that African country is not just another one on the long list of dictators that, unfortunately, we have on this planet, but I dare say that he is one of the top ten dictators.
I think that in the EU we have to do the impossible to make this person go and end his days sitting in front of a human rights court.
We therefore wish Zimbabwe a happy 2009 year without Mugabe.
We have a draft resolution on Nicaragua, in which we need to take into account different aspects.
Firstly, there are allegations of fraud, at least in two municipalities, Nicaragua and León, and this tarnishes the result of the election of 9 November.
On the other hand, we have a government that has been concerned with resolving some of the problems that undermine human rights in Latin America.
specifically poverty, illiteracy, infant mortality, access to drinking water and lack of land tenure rights for indigenous peoples.
So, when we look at the case, we have to distinguish perfectly between the two aspects.

On the one hand, we have to promote human rights and we have to defend human rights in the traditional sense, as political rights, and on the other hand, we have to encourage the Government of Nicaragua to continue with its function of helping the most needy sectors of the country.
Of course, we have to guarantee that the opposition can carry out its function, we have to oppose fraud and we have to defend the actions of the NGOs.
However, we need to have a balanced result.
The Socialist Group has tabled several oral amendments in an attempt to steer the final text so that it is more balanced with regard to the allegations of
violations of fundamental rights, on the criminal nature of the investigations and on the number of municipalities in which some kind of derogation has been applied.

In short, we want to condemn the alleged infringements of fundamental political rights, but without going so far as to place the Nicaraguan Government in a difficult situation that prevents it from continuing to carry out its function of supporting the most disadvantaged sectors of society.
on the situation created in Nicaragua by the attacks against some human rights defenders, against some of the fundamental freedoms and against democracy.

There is no need to mention here and now Soila América or Ernesto Cardenal or Carlos Mejía Godoy or Jarquín or Sergio Ramírez or so many other Nicaraguans who today feel betrayed in their hopes for a better Nicaragua.
In November 2006, Mr Ortega asked the Nicaraguans for, and democratically obtained, what he called a ‘new opportunity’.
Today we already know what he wanted it for.
Since then, arbitrariness, nepotism and corruption have taken root in the Government institutions, culminating in the electoral fraud of the 9 November elections.

Serious decisions had previously been taken, such as the cancellation of the legal status of some opposition parties and threats and intimidation of members of civil society, journalists and the media.
President Ortega described the European Union governments as flies swarming over garbage and described the external aid received by Nicaragua as crumbs.
He soon realised that there was not enough Venezuelan oil to do without that development aid.

When we are about to enter a decisive phase in the negotiations on an association agreement between the European Union and Central America, the Government of Nicaragua must know that it will always find an ally and a friend in us,

However, for us, the demand for respect for human rights, freedom of expression and democracy, in short, is a condition that we will not give up on, because we believe that the people of Nicaragua would especially benefit from it.
Mr President, on 9 November, municipal elections were held in Nicaragua.
As in previous occasions, the electoral process has been questioned in terms of democratic legitimacy, with accusations of fraud and the occurrence of
demonstrations and clashes between supporters of different political parties, resulting in numerous injuries and a deepening of the political crisis that the country has been suffering.
Two political parties were not allowed to stand in the elections, which is unacceptable because it infringes the fundamental right of all citizens to political participation.
On the other hand, we have received direct testimony from representatives of human rights organisations who have informed us that the civil society is questioning the judicial system.

on the existence of administrative repression mechanisms that have led to seventeen social movements organisations being taken to court, and on the persecution of independent journalists, even at the European Commission delegation.

For all of this, we want to raise our voice and make an appeal: first, to the political parties to condemn the acts of violence carried out by their supporters, and secondly, to the Government itself to re-open spaces for participation

citizens, that freedom of expression be respected, that marches no longer be banned and that all NGOs can receive aid from international cooperation, not just those from Venezuela for pro-government associations.
Likewise, they should repeal the penalisation of therapeutic abortion in cases where the mother ’ s life is in danger.

Finally, we also want to ask the Commission that the negotiations on the association agreement between Central America and the European Union remind Nicaragua that it must respect the principles of the rule of law and democracy.
democracy and human rights, all this without prejudice to Europe continuing to provide our development aid to the Nicaraguan people, whom we want to have the best possible future.
Madam President, I was going to refer to the Breyer report.
I must say that European farmers are fully aware that particular attention must be paid to human health and to the protection of the environment when using plant protection products.
However, the sector is deeply concerned that Parliament does not understand the impact that this regulation could have in the future.
There are impact assessments of their potential that point out that, due to the future scarcity of plant protection products, it will be very difficult for
control of pests and diseases affecting the cultivation of a large number of foods, specifically all Mediterranean products, as well as the cultivation of ornamental plants and cut flowers.
I am fully aware that the agreement reached in the trialogue has been the result of a hard negotiation and I cannot but recognise the work done by the rapporteurs.

However, we must bear in mind that the temporal definition of endocrine disrupters will result in the disappearance of a large number of active substances, especially insecticides, which are of key importance for agriculture.

Producers must have access to a sufficient number of active substances in order to effectively and safely combat any plant disease or pest, bearing in mind that the danger often lies not in the product, but in its misuse.
For all these reasons, the Spanish delegation of the PPE-DE Group supports the proposals calling for an impact assessment and will vote in favour of the amendments
by Mr Sturdy, which I myself signed, on endocrine disrupters and those that promote the availability of plant protection products in cases of emergency.

Moreover, if this regulation is adopted in the terms of the agreement reached, the effects will be a reduction in food production and a rise in prices, which will lead us to import those same foods that we are not producing, treated with the products that we are prohibiting.

Mr President, Mr Cappato, your report is a report that is full of goodwill. The most evident proof of this is that it did not merit any amendments during its consideration in the Committee on Civil Liberties, Justice and Home Affairs.
It was you who introduced 18 amendments to your own report.
And those amendments are the ones that have introduced a plus of radicalism, let's say so in general terms, into that report, which raises very serious doubts for me.
I would divide the amendments you have tabled into four distinct categories. I stress that you have tabled them yourself in a report that initially received the support of all the groups.
Some impossible. You ask for some things that are impossible.

For example, Mr Cappato, you are asking for things for which there is no legal basis yet. For example, until the Treaty of Lisbon enters into force, it will be impossible to harmonise the rules of the different committees.
something similar to what Commissioner Wallström has just said. Or you ask, for example, that we declare 2009 the year of transparency. We are late. The deadline has passed.
So you are asking for things that are impossible.
It also asks for other things that are not advisable.
For example, you are asking for transparency in the meetings of the political groups.
No, I don't.
that privacy is needed. Privacy is a political good.
If I want to disagree with my party, to maintain an opposition, I want to do it in private, and that is not a lack of transparency.
We must demand transparency in public bodies, but not within political parties, where debates must be guaranteed within privacy.
Therefore, I cannot follow you on that.
There are some other comments made by you, Mr Cappato, which are uncertain.
For example, you say that 90% of legislative activity is comitology.
That is a reckless exaggeration, Mr Capoto.
Furthermore, climatology implicitly includes certain transparency standards.

If what we are now going to do is create more mechanisms, more bureaucracy, more controls, we will be going against what we want to achieve, which is transparency, which is something that is very close to simplicity.
Finally, before the President calls me to order because I am running out of time, there are some things that are unnecessary.

For example, I understand that it is very popular and very attractive to public opinion to say that there should be transparency in the work of Members, that it should be known whether they attend the plenary sessions.
It's already known, Mr. Capacho.
Or they go to the committees, as we all know, and if there is a record of all of them, they are acts that are accessible to all ordinary people.
Or the debts they charge. We already know that, Mr. Cappato.
In other words, it is perfectly possible for a citizen to have access to that information.

Therefore, I understand that it is very popular with the public and that it looks good to defend transparency, but I want you to know that transparency means making things look as they are, and when one contributes to things not looking as they are, Mr Cappato, then we are not contributing to transparency.
Thank you for your generosity, Mr President.
the situation in Gaza with horror, pain and shame, but also with the commitment to defend peace and those who suffer most, and to maintain dignity and hope.
horror at the repeated scenes of murdered children and women torn apart in their endless suffering after the bombardments of the ghetto that Gaza has become.
The same mistake that Picasso painted in his Guernica, our Guernica, razed by the Junkers of the Condor Legion seven decades ago.
I share the pain of so many victims of so much suffering.
and shame at the inability of all of our European Union Member States and of the international community to prevent this criminal aggression and then to put an end to it.
I am ashamed and outraged by so much lying, so much ambiguity and so much empty talk.
We should be ashamed, because we know exactly what is going on and we are not acting with the necessary and required firmness and consistency.
History will therefore hold many people to account as accomplices in the crime, if nothing else, for omission.

Rajatabela must be complied with, just as we will have to comply with our Association Agreement with Israel, which provides for its suspension in the event of conduct such as that which is taking place.
By the way, Hamas is also responsible for the media blackout, which nobody has so far denounced.

We must tell Israel in no uncertain terms to stop killing, to allow the injured to be treated and the victims to be fed, and that its attitude towards international law has to have consequences for its relations with Europe.
I would like to greet the few young European co-operatives who are suffering with the people of Gaza, in particular Alberto Arce.
They represent the best of the values of solidarity and freedom of our Europe, which has to be consistent in this terrible conflict.

Madam President, Commissioner, as we can see, the textile sector has recently been suffering a serious crisis, which has led to numerous company closures, relocations and unemployment, all of which are concentrated in regions specialised in this activity.

In the current economic climate of crisis, the European Commission should act quickly, alongside the Member States, to alleviate the socio-economic effects of this restructuring, which are especially dramatic for the affected areas and families.
I think that we need to help textile and clothing workers with social measures and with plans for companies that are being restructured and which are currently in serious difficulties.
It would be desirable to allocate a substantial part of the Globalisation Adjustment Fund to the restructuring and retraining in the textile sector.
particularly to small and medium-sized enterprises, which make up the majority of the sector in the European Union and which suffer the effects of market liberalisation to a greater extent.
Furthermore, it is necessary to re-establish the import controls system, particularly for those imports from China, due to their volume.
We are not promoting trade barriers at all.
We need to compensate for the negative effects of such a major transformation. We should not forget that the European Union is the second largest exporter of textiles and clothing in the world.

, which means that we need to guarantee the best conditions for access to third country markets, which are essential for the future of the textile and clothing industry in the European Union and especially for SMEs.
All of this, of course, always guaranteeing fair competition on the basis of promoting environmental and social standards in these countries.

In this respect, the publication of correct information to consumers, for example, through the regulation on ' made in ' labelling, which, as far as we know, has not been implemented, would be very useful, as it
among other things, this would mean subjecting imported products to the same safety and consumer protection requirements as those manufactured within the European Union.
Mr Romeva, the Spanish Government, do not worry, will cooperate as it has always done.

I remind you that, in this matter, the Spanish Minister of Foreign Affairs, Mr Moratinos, was the first to appear before the committee of inquiry and the one who answered all the questions, including yours.

Mr President, this House has been denouncing for some time the torture and inhuman and degrading treatment being carried out at the military base at Guantánamo in the name of the fight against international terrorism.
We therefore welcome President Obama ’ s decision to suspend the trials for four months and his objective of closing the prison definitively within one year.
Now, although the responsibility lies with the US Government, the Union cannot turn a blind eye and must offer its cooperation in order to close the detention centre.
We should also consider the possibility of accepting prisoners from countries where human rights are not guaranteed, if the United States does not request it.

Then we will have to give a common European response, in which the Spanish Government will participate, respecting international legal framework and analysing, on a case-by-case basis, the legal situation of each citizen, of each detainee, the origin and detention and their situation.

Mr President, in the EU there are various cases of languages belonging to historic European peoples that, because they are not states, are not allowed to be used in the debates of this Parliament, and this is a democratic and representative deficit.

One of these cases is that of the Basque language, Euskera, which is not a minority language but an official language, at least in the southern part of the Basque Country, which is administratively part of the Spanish State.

However, the same does not happen – and please do not take this personally, Commissioner – with the Basque Country in the north, which is part of the French State, whose President, before the UN General Assembly, stated that denying respect to national identities and languages is to feed humiliation and that without that respect there will be no peace in the world.
Then again, neither Basque, Corsican, Breton nor Occitan have the slightest official recognition or support to respect or promote their use.

For this reason, I ask the Fundamental Rights Agency to work so that, within the Member States, the rights to the mother tongue of any European people are not violated, nor are its inhabitants discriminated against, and that all of them are considered official within their respective territories.
The most important thing is that the European Union is not left behind.

Madam President, first of all, I would like to thank our rapporteur, Mr Fava, for his great work in reaching a common position with the Council, which includes many improvements proposed by this House.
With this directive, we are once again demonstrating our commitment to building a common migration policy based on a global approach.
The approach of this directive is clear: to fight the mafias, punish unscrupulous employers and protect exploited immigrants who do not have any kind of social protection.
We want to see an end to the unjust, miserable wages for immigrants, which also distort average wages, especially in sectors such as construction, farming, domestic service and the hotel trade.
These measures require great courage and political will, as there are many vested interests and a lot of money moving in the underground economy.
Precisely in difficult times, it is more important than ever to manage migratory flows intelligently, with generosity but with responsibility.
It would be easy to give in to the temptation of not trying to tackle the underground economy.
We cannot look the other way and leave some eight million illegal immigrants defenceless in working conditions close to slavery.

Ladies and gentlemen, if we want this directive to be effective, we need rigorous inspections and economic penalties and even criminal penalties in the most serious cases, which will have a deterrent effect on business operators.
We will thus succeed in reducing the underground economy and cutting the migratory incentive created by the possibility of working irregularly.
It must be made clear that the only option for working in Europe is the legal route.
We therefore want to move forward with the global approach and we ask the Commission, Commissioner Barrot, to present the new ' blue cards ' for the other categories of worker as soon as possible.
Ladies and gentlemen, Mrs Ferrero-Waldner, Mr Solana, I would like to begin by thanking the three rapporteurs for their work, and as shadow rapporteur on the report on the CFP, I will refer to
this report in particular, starting by congratulating Mr Zaleski on the result of his work and on the collaboration that he has offered to the other groups in order to achieve a consensual result.

Just as this Parliament has called on the European Union on numerous occasions to equip itself with the necessary instruments to speak with one voice in the world, the House itself must also show cohesion in assessing and promoting the major priorities in foreign and security policy.

Our group, the Group of the Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats in the European Parliament, has been working in two directions. On the one hand, introducing or reinforcing major issues that are key for us, across the whole of foreign policy, such as combating climate change, promoting

peace throughout the planet or a commitment to human development. On the other hand, we should propose elements to rebalance the geographical priorities of the CFP, as they originally appeared in the text, if they did appear, or introduce them as new elements if they were absent.
We have therefore advocated, for example, greater clarification of the relations between the institutional frameworks and cooperation involved in the recently defined Eastern dimension.
We have defended greater diversification and greater relevance in relations with Africa, a continent that we often only remember when particularly violent wars break out, and often not even then.

With regard to Latin America, we wanted the current processes of negotiation of association agreements, the first of a regional nature that the European Union will conclude in its entire history, to be reflected.

As regards the Mediterranean, we have opposed a reductionist approach that only talks about security, in order to include, in its place, the whole rich political, economic and socio-cultural heritage of the Barcelona process.

As regards the amendments for the plenary, our group has not tabled any, believing that this in some way strengthens the balance achieved with the compromises, and therefore we are going to oppose the majority of the amendments so as not to weaken the compromise reached in the Committee on Foreign Affairs. Thank you.
Mr President, the referendum in Venezuela has ended with a victory for the ‘ yes ’, which has been gracefully recognised by the democratic opposition.

It is no less true that there was no equality of opportunity in the campaign and that the official party had the overwhelming support of the entire state apparatus, while the opposition suffered constant harassment and coercion.
Even under those conditions, the country has been practically split in two, and it will be very difficult to build the future with only one of them.
The European Union must promote dialogue, inclusion and consensus among the Venezuelan political and social actors for the good of the country.
It is not with visceral attitudes, disqualifications or insults that we will help Venezuela to find its way to a democratic, pluralist and free society.

In this respect, we criticise the Venezuelan Government's decision to expel a Spanish Member of the European Parliament, and even more so the way in which this expulsion was carried out, but we call on this House to avoid

that our representatives make statements during their visits to third countries that breach the local legislation in force and even less that they insult a head of state, however much he may be open to criticism.
With these attitudes, we are jeopardising future European Parliament missions to other countries.

Mr President, I have just returned from Venezuela, where I was invited by the National Electoral Council to take part as a member of an international electoral observation group in the referendum of 15 December.

The European members of that group submitted a report to the National Electoral Council, with an overall positive assessment of the process in terms of its organisation, transparency, participation, free and secret exercise of the right to vote and security at all stages.

Regarding the statements made by Mr Herrero to a Venezuelan television channel, I would like to point out that they were aimed at delegitimising the electoral process under way, they make serious accusations against the democratic institutions of that country and they border on interference in the internal politics of a sovereign country.
Our Parliament should not encourage any kind of confrontation with the democratic institutions of Venezuela.
On the contrary, it would be up to the honourable Member to explain to this House his actions, which affect all of us.
The Committee on Budgets considers that it is of the utmost importance to clearly define each of the budgetary instruments.
Therefore, the most realistic option seems to be the creation of a new instrument for actions other than official development assistance in countries covered by this regulation.
From a budgetary point of view, the proposal for financing from the Development Committee does not seem appropriate, as this money does not exist, and
not to have those lines with a quantity of credit assigned on a multiannual basis. There is a credit for 2009, but not for any further years.
In any case, as the funding of this new cooperation instrument must be compatible with the 2007-2013 financial framework, it is appropriate
I would like to stress the importance of the mid-term review of the financial framework, which should allow for an adjustment of the maximum ceilings of the different headings.
Madam President, at this late hour on a Monday, the Commission probably has other things on its mind than this report.
In short, all we are doing is replying to a Commission report, a report on the application of Regulation (EC) No 1206 / 2001 of the
Council of 28 May 2001 on cooperation between the judicial authorities of the Member States in the taking of evidence in civil or commercial matters.
We are in an area that is not really a Community one, but an intergovernmental one, in which the Council, through the regulation, has tried to stimulate cooperation between those judicial bodies.
But in doing so, I think that the European Union is being somewhat lax.

That is to say, certain measures have been foreseen, for example, the issuing of practical guides, the use of electronic media, etcetera, but I recognise that it is quite difficult for the Commission to take action

I do not know what the Commission has achieved or intends to achieve, but it seems to me that it will be very difficult to achieve it, given the enormous difficulty posed by this form of intergovernmental cooperation.

We are asking the judicial authorities to cooperate with each other. We are talking about 27 countries, judicial authorities that handle 22 different languages, with very different legal systems, and we are trying to achieve results.
The Commission has found that the results are not good, that there is a huge delay in these cooperation mechanisms and, of course, that we do not have the means to do so.
Ideally, we would have the material resources,
For example, what could the Commission do to equip the courts, especially those at lower levels, with technological equipment such as a video-conferencing system?
Videoconferencing may be a utopia for many small courts throughout the European Union, but it seems to be the only way to do it.
I think the European Union should rely on institutions that already exist, specifically the European Judicial Network.
We would have to strengthen this institution so that the judges themselves, through their cooperation systems, could carry out this type of communication.
On the other hand, any training of judges is fundamental.
This is something that the Commission cannot do on its own either.
However, the Commission can encourage the development of this type of training courses in Community law and in knowledge of the different legal systems.
Then there is a huge difficulty, which is the problem of languages. That is to say, how can a Spanish judge communicate with a Finnish judge, with two languages so different?
Judges cannot be expected to have to deal with their own law, which is already quite complex in itself, and also be required to have knowledge of this type of code.
What can we do from a practical point of view to make it easier for judges to interpret and translate?
What could we do to help them do it?
to present this report, to express no kind of rigor or harshness towards the Commission.
I think the work is quite difficult.
What I would like to know, taking advantage of the fact that we have here a Commissioner who is interested in this issue, is what the Commission thinks can be done to give greater

rigour to this regulation from 2001, which is a little vague, a little arbitrary, and I do not know whether, from a practical point of view, it can have any consequences or whether we can make progress with it.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I am very pleased to be able to conclude this parliamentary term with the final adoption of the last of the maritime packages.

However, I would like to recall that when I arrived at the European Parliament in 1999, we suffered a major maritime accident, the Erika shipwreck off the French coast, which gave rise to an initial set of legislative proposals aimed at preventing accidents.
We thought we had averted the danger, but shortly afterwards the coasts of Galicia were ruined by the tar from another accident, even worse than the first, that of the tanker

Since then, in the Committee on Transport and Tourism, we have been working on successive legislative packages, trying to overcome the excessive resistance we have encountered from shipowners, oil companies, classification societies and also certain Member States, which rejected our proposals because they considered them to be too demanding.

Over the past ten years, we have adopted various directives and regulations, some of which we have had to revise because, as a result of the cuts made by the Council, they were ineffective in their first draft.

We have adopted the rules for the introduction of double-hulled vessels, the European Fund for Major Oil Spills, ports of refuge, and now we are reviewing and adopting the liability of passenger carriers by sea, the investigation of

maritime accidents, the Community system for the monitoring and information on maritime traffic, control by the port State and, finally, the regulations and directives relating to ship inspection and recognition organisations.
All of this is good and desirable, and should enter into force and be applied by all Member States in the shortest possible time.
I am therefore satisfied.

However, the parliamentary term is coming to an end, I am leaving Parliament and I am going with the regret of not having succeeded in making it mandatory to introduce monitoring systems, which already exist and
are patented, in order to know when and how much waste from ballast tanks and fuel tanks has been illegally dumped into the sea by a particular ship.
In other words, a kind of black box or speedometer that maritime authorities can inspect every time a ship arrives at port.

I think we should pay more attention to the marine environment and adopt stricter procedures to stop the pollution we produce, which, if we do not stop, will affect our food chain and our lives, making us pay a high price.
I trust that we will not have to wait another ten years before we can control illegal marine discharges more effectively and efficiently.

Mr President, the revision of the Eurovignette Directive is an issue of great importance that concerns and worries us all, especially the peripheral countries, which feel that they are victims of this proposal.
The European Commission has decided to tackle the internalisation of the external costs of road transport by allocating three new costs: air pollution, noise pollution and congestion.

While the principles behind the proposal, 'the polluter pays' and 'the user pays', are reasonable, the solution proposed is not at all reasonable, as it will be discriminatory towards a sector that has been suffering the disastrous effects of the economic crisis for months.
To demonise road freight transport, or that it was not the objective, has turned out to be the end result.

This proposal, ladies and gentlemen, is ill-timed and, far from achieving the expected objectives of sustainable transport, would be the death knell for a large number of small and medium-sized European enterprises employing thousands of people.

people in the European Union who contribute daily to the distribution of goods, ensuring that products reach their final consumer, a consumer who will see his products become more expensive as a result of these charges.

Ladies and gentlemen, we need a transport system that is competitive, sustainable and respectful of the environment, without neglecting the road, because at present it is the only way of getting everywhere.
Modal shift is still a dream, far from being a reality.
The trans-European networks, the motorways of the sea and the cross-border connections are still, in some cases, projects rather than reality.
Ladies and gentlemen, when the global economy ’ s Titanic sinks, it is not possible to ask the orchestra to keep playing, and certainly not for pleasure.

Mr President, on 15 July 2008, the European Commission adopted a communication addressed to the Council and to Parliament recommending the establishment of a strategic partnership between the European Union and Mexico.
For its part, the European Council on Foreign Relations recognised Mexico as a strategic partner on 13 October, pending Parliament's opinion on the matter.
Let us remember that, at the seventh meeting of the EU-Mexico Parliamentary Committee, held at the end of October last year, we said that a
The strategic partnership between Mexico and the European Union is a necessary boost to strengthen and develop the true potential of our bilateral relations.
We point out the good state of relations between the two sides and the positive balance of the current global agreement.

We stress the need for our respective executives to strengthen collaboration in political, economic and cooperation terms to the maximum, in the face of the new challenges of all kinds arising from the financial and economic crisis shaking our continents.

We share values and objectives with the great country that is Mexico, such as the development of democratic forms of government, the commitment to gender equality, the consolidation of the rule of law, equitable and sustainable development and respect for human rights.
We advocate closer cooperation in combating organised crime, terrorism and drug trafficking, based on the principles of shared responsibility and strict adherence to international law.
Consequently, we are going to support the proposal for recommendations on the EU-Mexico strategic partnership, which will be submitted to Parliament this morning.
Madam President, with regard to the report on micro-credit, I would like to congratulate all the rapporteurs because they have done a great job.
The Commission initiative has undoubtedly improved.
I believe that the recommendations established here are going to allow us to lay the foundations for developing a genuine framework in the European Union for the micro-credit sector, a sector
has been successful in many developing countries and even in some European countries, both as an economic activity mechanism and as an instrument to improve social inclusion and job creation.

but it had not been transferred to the sector or to the Community context. I believe that this is an opportunity to do so, particularly given the economic and financial situation in which we find ourselves.
It is a priority to strengthen the initiatives being advanced here, but we need to take further steps.
The funds available for this microcredit support structure must be increased.
We need to make it easier for people and companies that do not have access to loans to obtain them, and in this respect, Madam President, I would like to highlight the example of the
the European guarantee for micro-credit loans as an instrument that could facilitate that access and that it has been included in the report.
I will conclude by saying that this will undoubtedly lay the foundations for us to create a harmonised framework in the European context that promotes the micro-credit sector.

Madam President, given that we are talking about the budget, I think we should bear in mind that next year we should negotiate the continuation of the aid for coal, an indigenous source of energy that is essential.
I say this in good time, because some senior official, inappropriately, confused public opinion by daring to express an opinion to the contrary, leaking it to an economic publication.
None of that.
We will have to continue supporting the coal industry in my country after 2012, and I want that to be on the Minutes.
Officials, please limit yourselves to implementing the current plan and then, from 2012, to implementing the instrument that we will begin to negotiate with the sector next year.
Mr President, Commissioner, this question, as Mr Boklan has explained, is very timely, given the diplomatic conference on the ship recycling convention, which is to be held in May.
For this reason, I would like to congratulate Mr Blokland not only on the question, but also on the resolution that he has prepared.

The EPP Group supports the fight against the bad practices and methods being used to scrap ships, and therefore supports Mr Blokland ’ s motion for a resolution. However, there are some points that concern us.

The first is paragraph 3, which he also mentioned, stating that ships in their entirety should be considered hazardous waste and, as a consequence, should fall within the scope of the Basel Convention.

We have tabled an amendment on this section because there are different interpretations within and outside the European Union, and we believe that the Basel Convention must be taken into account and not contradicted, but it should be the future international agreement on ship waste that defines the details of its application.
The draft resolution also calls in paragraph 14 for a financing mechanism based on mandatory contributions from the maritime transport sector.

We feel that it is a little premature to talk about compulsory contributions at this time, when the WTO has not yet decided what system will be adopted for this funding, and it seems to me that the European Commission does not have a clear answer on this matter either.
Finally, paragraph 15 calls on the Commission to clearly establish that the Member State with jurisdiction over the waste is the responsible State.
We do not think it is the Commission ’ s responsibility to decide this, as it should also be decided in an international agreement.

The important thing is to ensure consistency between the future ship recycling convention and the Basel Convention on the transboundary movement of hazardous wastes and their disposal, and all this taking into account not only the Basel Convention but all existing legislation.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, the first thing I would like to do is to congratulate the rapporteur, with whom we share a great deal of agreement on this programme, and to congratulate ourselves, because, in times of economic crisis, it is essential to maintain a programme such as this, which has clear positive social effects.

Although this is a consultation procedure from the European Parliament, we must send a clear political signal that, at this time of economic uncertainty, we in Brussels and Strasbourg are not solely concerned with

not only concerned with a recovery of the financial system, but we are fully aware that social policies, especially those that support those most in need, the poorest people in the European Union, have to grow.

This food distribution programme should continue to be considered part of the common agricultural policy – we agree with the Commission on this – not only because European agriculture has a strong social dimension, but also because it is a working instrument, because it is needed more than ever and because it must continue to function.

The Socialist Group, like the rapporteur, opposes the co-financing of this programme between the European Union and the Member States, as this would discriminate between states according to their wealth, and it could mean that the programme could not be implemented in the countries most in need.

It is incomprehensible that, precisely when it is most needed, the Commission should want to save Community money on the most social aspect of the CAP, particularly given that there are always budget surpluses in the agricultural budget.
Full EU funding is essential for us.
We need to ensure that this programme reaches all countries and, above all, the most needy.
Forty-three million potential beneficiaries of this programme are demanding greater effort from us, and in no case should we cut budget expenditure.
Austerity, yes, but not with the poorest.
It is precisely to guarantee that it reaches citizens in all Member States that the programme must cover the entire journey of the food distributed.

For this reason, the Socialist Group, for which I am rapporteur, has tabled an amendment to ensure that all costs related to distribution, including transport, storage and administrative costs, are covered by Community funds.
We also agree with the rapporteur that food should be preferably from the Community and of good quality.
It is a position consistent with that taken by this Parliament in other similar programmes that we discussed here recently, such as the school fruit distribution plan.
Nothing more, and thank you very much.
We hope that the Commission takes account of Parliament ’ s position when it votes and, above all, that it implements the plan in the European Union as soon as possible.
The Arctic region is one of the most fragile regions on our planet.
The consequences of unrestricted exploitation of its natural resources would be catastrophic, not only for the immediate environment and the indigenous population, but also for the world as a whole.

The thawing of vast areas has brought these risks to the fore, and so a new, globally applicable regulation is needed to protect the Arctic, similar to the one that already exists for the Antarctic, even bearing in mind the differences that have already been highlighted in the debate.

An international treaty is needed between all the parties involved, including the European Union, to protect this unique environment, to ensure that all human activities are fully sustainable and to multilaterally regulate navigation through the new maritime routes that are becoming accessible.
The Arctic Council, during its years of existence, has been a model of cooperation for managing common problems.

At times like these, when we are facing difficulties and uncertainty, we must raise that spirit and that harmony to a higher level, so as to prevent the States in the region or other international actors from getting involved in geostrategic squabbles, forgetting what should be our common goal: the preservation of a great shared heritage.
Mr President, unlike the two speakers who have just preceded me, I agree with the majority of those who have spoken.
This is a good directive, an excellent directive, aimed at protecting interpreters.
When we talk about having to look for a smarter way of doing business, what we are really talking about is doing away with intellectual property.
because business can only be done if there is established law.
We already have a right established at national level.
The Commission, under the leadership of Mr McCreevy, has adopted a courageous initiative, a positive initiative, and I believe that this House should adopt the compromise that we have
achieved in the Committee on Legal Affairs, thanks to the work of the rapporteur, Mr Crowley, and of the different shadow rapporteurs, such as Mrs McGuinness and Mr Toubon.
I think it is a magnificent directive, a directive that will promote the creation of intellectual property.
Moreover, that directive includes a number of recommendations regarding the continuation of the work.

I believe that the adoption of that report by this House, and I hope that the Council will follow the lines of the Council, the Commission and Parliament, will help to ensure that in the next legislature the

Commission to continue along these lines, to make proposals to Parliament and to move forward in the area of intellectual property protection, which is essential for the development of our European Union as a great institution based on a common culture.

Mr President, the information you have given me is correct, but the impression we Members of the European Parliament, and ultimately the European citizens, have is that we have a very dangerous loophole in Community law.
At the moment, as fiscal requirements are being tightened in each of the Member States, mobility is almost impossible.
And when we ask the Commission, the reason for the pragmatic approach is that the Commission never finds support in the Council.

We are in a kind of vicious circle, in which the Commission refers us to the Council and the Council refers us to the Commission. But meanwhile, the reality is that the citizens who live in this Europe that we want to build do not have the possibility of living in different countries or establishing relationships as a result of the heavy tax burden that the lack of fiscal harmonisation can entail.
Can the Council do anything to get us out of this vicious circle?
Madam President, for once I am pleased that the Commissioner is not here, but that Mr Tajani has come, because Mr Tajani has the advantage of having been a Member of this House.
As a Member of the European Parliament, I know that you have experienced the frustration that we Members of the European Parliament feel with regard to the application of Community law.

Now, in Parliament, we tend to get involved with the Commission. I think we are asking the Commission to carry out the impossible, because all Community law and all application of Community law is based on indirect application.

The Commission has only a few officials in its headquarters, where it receives some complaints and has some possibilities for action. But at the moment, the trend is to limit budgetary powers, with the result that the Commission will not have the possibility of doing so.
All Community law, all application of Community law, is based on the actions of national authorities: national parliaments, national courts, national officials.
On this point, I do not think we can expect too much from the Commission.
What we have to do is help the Commission, and I believe that in Mrs Frassoni ’ s report there are some points that can be useful in achieving this implementation.
I am referring to those relating to the correlation of directives, the collaboration of national parliaments and the action of national courts.
Mr President, two years ago, on a mission led by my colleague and group member, Marianne, I had the opportunity to visit
Sierra Leone and to be present in this court and to be aware of the immense work that this court was doing, not only for Sierra Leone but for all of humanity.
The Special Court for Sierra Leone, as has already been said here, has of course set a precedent.

It is a precedent because, as the resolution rightly states, it is the first international court financed by voluntary contributions, the first court to be established in the country where the alleged crimes took place and it is also the first court, as has already been pointed out, to try a former Prime Minister.

For all these reasons, not only because it is a precedent, but also because it is a reference for other courts that have been created in its wake, such as those for Rwanda, former Yugoslavia, Cambodia or Lebanon, we believe that it is essential that this resolution, which we have been working on together with the Court for several months, be adopted.
Two years ago, we adopted a resolution to support its funding. Two years ago, the Court was going through a difficult period, during which it was running out of
money and did not have enough support. We must also thank the European Commission here for coming forward to support this court financially.

Now, we are asking for two things above all. First, that the people convicted serve their sentences. What is at stake is not just the operation of the Court, which will end next year, but the legacy that this Court will leave us. Secondly, obviously, all of this has to be accompanied by greater funding.
In short, the Special Court for Sierra Leone is an example and a reference for all of us and for all the courts that have judged war crimes.

It is an example, a reference and a lesson that the second poorest country on the planet is teaching us, in which, when we entered the court, we saw a slogan written that said, 'No justice, no peace'.
And precisely for that reason, we have a moral obligation, not only as Europeans but also as human beings, to ensure that the legacy of this Court remains permanent in history.
Mr President, I will be very brief, which I am sure you will appreciate at this time of night. Mr Barrot, thank you once again, because you have shown, as you did in the third package,

maritime sector, and that your political group has been able to drive an agreement through the Council and make it possible for Parliament, the Council and the Commission to reach an agreement on this matter in these final days of the parliamentary term.
I think we should congratulate ourselves.
I certainly appreciate the close cooperation that the rapporteurs for opinion from both the Committee on Legal Affairs and the Committee on the Environment have had.

I thank and repeat the work and cooperation of the shadow rapporteurs and, in short, I believe that tomorrow we will adopt an agreement at first reading on a regulation so important that it undoubtedly completes the work of the

third maritime package is a success for Parliament and for the European Union, for the three institutions, and justifies once again the codecision procedure, which is useful and allows a constructive dialogue in favour of all the citizens of the European Union.
I was short of a minute, so I would like to answer the Commissioner, who knows of my friendship and affection for her and, indeed, of my friendship and affection for Mr Orbán.
But the Commissioner has not said a single word about the Ombudsman ’ s recommendation.
The Ombudsman, who knows all the arguments that the Commissioner has given us, has made a specific recommendation that the Commission seems to be ignoring in all its aspects.

I do not even want Ms Kuneva to answer me, because clearly she is not the one who has the data, but all the data she has given me are in line with what we knew and with what the Ombudsman also knows, and she does not tell us whether, in fact,

Despite all that, and as the Ombudsman, who is aware of that, recommends that the rule be changed, you do not tell us whether the Commission is going to take on board what the Ombudsman is asking for and what Parliament recommends, based on what the Ombudsman has asked for.
Mr President, I believe that the Commission proposals for a regulation are important and necessary, and on the other hand it was also important and necessary that the

European Parliament, we should insist on the principles on which the two rapporteurs of the European Parliament, Mr Väyrynen and Mr de Prá, have insisted, and they are the principle of Community competence.

This is a matter of Community competence, in which, for practical reasons, it is advisable to maintain certain powers for the Member States, but, as Mr Barrot said, these should be limited in scope and duration.
The Community's powers are irrevocable and neither the Council, the Commission nor Parliament has the power to waive those Community powers.

Having said that, this is an exceptional procedure. I believe that the amendments we have discussed and tabled, and which I believe will be adopted by Parliament tomorrow, will allow this package of measures to be adopted at first reading.

But I also hope that, in future, the Commission will build on this and that we can make progress in drawing up a genuine European private law, which is becoming increasingly necessary, as Mr Sógor stressed in his last speech.
We are talking about the problems that affect people most directly and, to the extent that we are in a position to solve people's problems, people will realise that the European Union has a function.
In short, Mr President, I would like to congratulate my colleagues, Mr Dabrowski and Mr Vieser, and to thank the Commission and the Council for their willingness to collaborate with us on this issue.

Madam President, Commissioner, I have signed this resolution and asked to speak in this House because I was a witness to the so-called expulsion, but in fact the abduction, of our colleague, Luis Herrero.

Moreover, I was able to see that political persecution, abuse of power by the Chávez regime, intimidation of opponents, threats, contempt for human dignity and the use of the courts as a weapon are common practice in Venezuela, among other things.

Manuel Chaves ' s case is the straw that broke the camel ' s back and has led to this resolution, but there are thousands of cases as serious as his that are cited in the resolution and others that are not.
This is the case of Ilio Cedeño, who was born in a humble district of Caracas, Petare, well known for its dangerousness and also because it voted for a mayor who was not from the Chavista regime.
Eligio studied thanks to the help of others, and specifically Citibank, and managed to found his own business, the Banco de Caracas, and then lived a normal life, helping the most needy.
Today, however, he is serving a sentence in a Caracas prison, illegally, after two years of detention without any credible charges.
Their only crime: being part of the economic oligarchy.

Another case is that of Nisson Moreno, a student leader at the University of the Andes, who has been elected several times to the University Council and is President of the Federation of University Centres.
He won the 2003 elections to the Federation, the current Minister of the Interior and Justice, and this is his crime.
Today, he is accused of attempted murder, violent acts and all that despite having been cleared of all charges.

Situations such as yours are commonplace in this country, where persecuting opponents with the intention of excluding them from political life and discouraging dissent has become part of everyday life.

However, from here, we need to send a message of hope to Venezuelan democracy: despite the difficulties, I am sure that democracy will prevail and that President Chávez will be removed from office at the ballot box.
Madam President, Commissioner, it is true that progress has been made in recent times, but despite all the progress made, the South of the Union is burning and is burning more and more.
Therefore, the resolution that Parliament is proposing cannot be just another resolution that merely demonstrates the great concern that we feel about other people ’ s misfortunes.
Forest fires are caused by one thing or another, but nobody can deny that they are fuelled by climate change.

Regardless of any responsibilities that may exist in certain Member States or certain autonomous communities, it is clear that Parliament must clearly call on the Commission to act swiftly and to use all the resources at its disposal, which are not few.
The victims, the damage suffered and the need for reforestation do not understand bureaucracy or budgetary deadlines.
We need to prioritise and we need to act, and in this case, acting means modifying strategies, mobilising resources, increasing them and optimally coordinating preventive policies, demanding that the

responsibility on those Member States that lack them, but assuming that this is not a problem for some southern European countries and that climate change does not affect only some countries, but rather affects everyone and is a common cause.
Therefore, one of the main tasks for this Parliament, in the near future, must be to ensure that the European Union secures clear and binding commitments at the next summit in Copenhagen.
resolute in order to reduce greenhouse gas emissions to the maximum extent possible, because they are clearly at the root of the fires, and these in turn are the cause of further climate change.
