Madam Commissioner, you ended your speech by telling us that there is a crisis in the dairy sector, but you began by telling us that you had already taken all the measures and that the matter was sorted out.
Look, to rectify is wise.
That is a Spanish saying that could be applied very well to the case we are dealing with today.

The dairy sector has been suffering one of the worst crises in its history for months, and despite this, the European Commission or some governments, including, it seems, my own, the Spanish Government, are refusing to back down on the decisions regarding the health check of the CAP.
These decisions were adopted without taking into account the fragility of this sector and in a very different market context from that which emerged after that agreement was closed.
It seems that the Member States either did not want or could not see the storm that was approaching at that time.
The reform became outdated in a short space of time due to the huge shift in the market, a shift which shows that the sector is totally at the mercy of price volatility.

The fact that major producers such as France and Germany, countries that achieved the greatest increase in their national production quotas in that agreement, are now asking for a review of the decisions taken on the checks is food for thought.

In my opinion, the Member States were wrong and should have listened more carefully to the voices, including mine, that were calling for any final decision on the future of the sector to be postponed until 2011.
It is a pity that they did not listen to us.
Listen to them. Do it now.
Mr President, this Parliament is the palace of chimeras, the palace of dreams.
The Irish people, with their vote, with their strong vote, give us encouragement and strength to continue prospering and building a strong Europe and, above all, a federal Europe.
Only in this way, only in this way would we deserve to be called the legitimate heirs of Europe ’ s founding fathers, for whose work we are all here today.

Mr President, now that we have overcome the concern caused by the adoption of the Treaty of Lisbon, I believe it is time to consider how we should deal in future with the nationalist attitudes that so hinder European integration.

The constitutional breakdown in Honduras breaks two decades of no coups in Latin America and sets a perverse precedent for other countries, especially the weaker ones, such as Paraguay at present.
The European Parliament cannot and must not remain deaf and dumb in the face of such a violation of the rule of law.

Both the Council and the Commission have clearly condemned the coup and supported President Arias ’ mediation, in line with the position adopted unanimously by the OAS with the support of the United Nations.

The self-appointed President, Mr Micheletti, has failed to comply with the recently reached agreements, making a mockery of the international community and preventing, among other things, the return of the deposed President, Mr Zelaya.

Under the current circumstances, the election of a new President in the elections scheduled for 29 November is inherently illegitimate and the international community cannot and must not recognise the results.

The European Parliament would make a serious mistake if it sent an observation delegation to the elections, because this would be interpreted as a seal of approval for the coup d ’ état. The OAS has already ruled out sending an observation mission to these elections.
Parliament has always been a guarantor of freedom, democracy, the rule of law and respect for human rights.
As Vaclav Havel said this morning, there cannot be two weights or two measures.

We are all democrats here and, therefore, activists for freedom and, as such, whether we are talking about Cuba, China, North Korea or Honduras, our discourse must be firm in the face of those who trample on freedom.
Let us not be blinded by our ideological preferences.
There are no right-wing or left-wing coups, only liberticides and repressors.
We are very afraid that things will not change in Honduras and that a very dangerous precedent will have been set and the doctrine of the good, temporary, and not too bloody coup will have triumphed.

Mr President, I would like to congratulate the members of the Committee on the Environment, Public Health and Food Safety and its Chair, Mr Leinen, for the work they have done to enable us to have a resolution such as this one, which we must adopt unanimously.

and two underlying aspirations: one is to start working earlier rather than later on having a common energy policy, just as we once created a common agricultural policy because it was needed; and the other, which is of course also included, is to restore citizens' confidence in politics and in politicians, of which we have no shortage in Europe.
In that respect, the success of the summit will be the success of these aspirations.
I therefore encourage the President-in-Office of the Council to continue with his optimism, to put pressure on and negotiate as much as he can in order to bring others along to this success.

I do not know whether the leaders of this multipolar world want to be judged by history for being unable to reach a binding agreement, but as a Member and I believe that all representatives of the people cannot be judged by history for having failed to reach the binding commitment that we have with humanity to avoid climate change in Copenhagen.

Today, there is no doubt about the European Union's commitment to combating terrorism, and thanks to the Stockholm Programme, we will continue to make progress in this area of freedom, justice and security, which is so necessary.

The day this happens, no one in this Parliament or in any other will defend terrorists, as has happened, is happening and may continue to happen, with the consequent damage to the victims of terrorism, who see how those who murdered their loved ones are treated as heroes, while they suffer in silence.

Madam President, I would like to begin by congratulating Mrs McGuinness on this initiative, which highlights the important role played by the CAP in meeting the needs of international food supplies.

Now that the European Commission seems to want to reduce the weight of the CAP within the Community budget, we must say very clearly that the CAP is no longer a priority, but food self-sufficiency should be. In past decades, we have seen that, without the CAP, food self-sufficiency is very difficult, if not impossible, to achieve.
Agriculture cannot therefore be compared to other sectors of the economy capable of prospering in a free market, because the food market is not a free market.

Farmers need the support of the European Union in order to run their businesses, and the Union, in turn, needs farmers in order to maintain an agricultural model capable of providing sufficient, quality food to an increasingly demanding citizen.
I therefore believe that the CAP should change course, but not disappear.

To this end, direct aid to farmers would need to be guaranteed, as well as the re-establishment of a policy for managing agricultural markets that would provide greater price stability, which would benefit not only farmers, but also consumers and third countries.

We need to establish a framework of best practice to promote balanced relationships between the various agents in the food chain, avoiding abusive practices and promoting a fairer distribution of commercial margins.
Furthermore, we need a policy to inform European consumers, highlighting the efforts made by EU producers to comply with EU legislation on the environment.
environment, food security and animal welfare, because EU producers have to compete with production from third countries, where much lower standards are applied to imports.
Producers in third countries prefer to export to the European Union rather than supply their own domestic markets, simply because of profitability, with the backing of the WTO negotiations.
According to a United Nations report, in 2009, around 270 000 people were victims of human trafficking in the European Union.
With these figures in mind, EU action must primarily be based on the protection of victims, with special attention to the situation of women and children, who are the most vulnerable.
We cannot continue to stand by indifferently to this theatre, this spectacle of sexual exploitation that is taking place before our eyes.
For that reason, I fully support Parliament's request that assistance to victims be unconditional, that it be more forceful and that penalties be more severe, as Mr Barrot has just told us.
Having said that, I would like to ask the President and the Council to use all the European legislation in their hands, both existing and future, to protect the victims of human trafficking.

I believe that the proposal for a European protection order for victims, which I personally requested of the Spanish Presidency during the debates on the Stockholm Programme and which has finally come to light, will be an effective instrument for combating these crimes.

I hope that the Spanish Presidency will make a firm commitment, as we have already seen, to ensure that this order provides victims with special protection measures that are effective throughout the territory of the European Union.

I hope that those of us who have the responsibility of acting in the face of the most serious problems in our society give a real and concrete response to this extremely serious problem of human trafficking, and that we do not stop at good words.
We truly owe it to all the victims.
Mr Zapatero, for a Spaniard such as myself, it is an honour to welcome you here in your capacity as President-in-Office of the Union.
There are many expectations and challenges in this period, and of course we cannot fail to live up to the expectations created.
I would like the public to see that our renewed institutions are capable of providing solutions to real problems.
The programme proposed by your Lordships is one that we share.
that for the Popular Party of Spain, this is a matter of state, and that we will support you in everything that we see as being in the common interests of Europe and important for Spain.
It is an ambitious programme and one that I hope will be filled with content.
I would like people to see in that album of the Spanish Presidency, which will surely be colourful and varied, that in each event they see a milestone on real problems that affect them.
The crisis is hitting the economies and unemployment seems like the fifth horse of the Apocalypse.
You had the honesty to acknowledge that 20% unemployment exists in Spain, but that does not incapacitate you.
What I think is that it forces us to produce a solution together to a problem that we really have to solve together.
This is a real problem.
Mr President, I do not have time to say many things.

On the other hand, the globalised world – you referred to this intelligently – is giving rise to new centres of power, and it is necessary for the European Union to play a more active and consistent role in defending its values and interests.

The resolution of Parliament of 2 February 2006 recommends that Member States adopt a zero tolerance attitude towards all forms of violence against women and adopt the necessary measures to ensure better protection for victims.
The Stockholm Programme, adopted by this House, established a space of freedom, justice and security for all European citizens, and in it, combating gender-based violence is considered a priority.

It reflected my request to the Spanish Presidency to promote, during its term of office, a European protection order for victims of gender violence, to ensure that victims of these crimes enjoy the same level of protection in all Member States.

In a Europe without borders, the fight against gender violence should also have no borders, and Member States should make an effort to harmonise their legislation so that the idea of combating violence against women transcends the

legislative obstacles and, in the end, we could offer, at least within the European Union, protection for the lives of women and their children. I therefore ask the Commission and the Council to do everything

Fidel Castro and his oligarchy have long since put an end to the ideals of the Cuban revolution, which echoed so widely and inspired so many people throughout the world, especially the international youth.
What characterises the Castro brothers ' dictatorship at the moment, however, is the lack of piety towards political prisoners and prisoners of conscience, the lack of piety towards their own people.
It reminds me of a dying Franco, with the ill-treatment of political prisoners, whom he also considered common, and his own obsessive contempt for exiles.
Castro ends up like Franco and all the enemies of freedom when they seize power.
But we must save lives and show solidarity with those who are fighting and suffering.
It is our duty as Europeans committed to democracy, freedom and human rights.
Madam President, President of the Council, Commissioner, never again, never again should we allow our silence to become complicity with the liberticides.

Let us never again allow a person who was fighting for his rights and for everyone else's rights to die in a Cuban jail or in any other jail without us raising our voice firmly and demanding his salvation.

Mr Orlando Zapata Tamayo, a 42-year-old Afro-Cuban bricklayer who was only asking for better prison conditions, died after 86 days on hunger strike and seven years in jail for demanding respect for human rights.
During those seven years, he was maltreated, humiliated and tormented by his jailers, in the midst of – we must remember – a thick silence from the international community.
Other prisoners and human rights activists in Cuba are now on hunger strike, such as the psychologist and journalist Guillermo Fariñas.

Ladies and gentlemen, the resolution that we are debating today and will vote on tomorrow, and which I am presenting on behalf of the Group of the Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats in the European Parliament, calls for the release of all political prisoners in Cuba.
We must once again speak out in favour of a democratic transition and of strict respect for fundamental human rights.
Let us not, for the moment, enter into a subject that divides us: whether the common position should be maintained or not.
Let us now focus on the issue of saving lives and on the issue of human rights.
We need a period of calm reflection in which we can find common ground on future policy with Cuba.
But this text also has an important added value.
The fact that six political groups have tabled it expresses a new era of broad consensus in this Chamber on the subject of human rights.

that the European democratic right is never again said to be 'bending the knee' to 'Yankee imperialism', but that social democrats are never again said to be complacent or complicit with communist dictatorships.
Simply because both statements are false, and the victims of the liberticide, wherever they are, should know that they will find us united in the unconditional defence of their cause.

Finally, I would also like to thank the President of the Spanish Government and President of the European Union, Mr Rodríguez Zapatero, for his encouragement and support in bringing forward this resolution that we are debating today.

I pay tribute to the poet Raúl Rivero in the last verse of his poem 'Havana': 'Do not impose taxes on affection, on emptiness, on suffocation, on bitterness'.
The ruins of the homeland are safe.
Calm down, colleagues.

This week, International Women's Day was celebrated.
In this Parliament, we had many posters in all the languages of the European Union saying that yes, we can end violence against women.

Today we are talking about violence in Mexico, and I think it is only fair to acknowledge that Mexican women, indigenous and non-indigenous, are the least protected victims of local and federal authorities.
Mexican women are victims of all forms of violence, from domestic violence to sexual violence and torture perpetrated by members of the army or the police, and even murder.
Unfortunately, so far the responses from the Mexican authorities have been ineffective and, therefore, it is quite rightly said that there is almost total impunity.
Our resolution calls on the Mexican Government to combat femicides, a harsh but correct word, when we see that almost all murders of women go unpunished.
For example, in the State of Mexico, in the last four years, only 11% of the criminals who murdered almost 700 women were convicted.
At the same time, it seems that women are the judicial system ’ s preferred victims.
There is no other way to explain how it was possible that two indigenous women, Alberta Alcántara and Teresa González, were sentenced to twenty-one years in prison and even
They have now spent more than three years in jail for holding six Federal Investigation Agency men hostage during an incident at a market.
investigation. I hope that the judge who will decide on the appeal in this case in a week will realise how ridiculous and unjust the situation is. But violence against women starts in the family.

I hope that the Mexican legislators will very soon improve the General Law on Women's Access to a Life Free from Violence so that the types of violence and the mechanisms that make the rules operational can be defined.
Likewise, it would be normal to establish that domestic violence and rape within marriage are criminal offences.
Moreover, in order for Mexican society to change and reject violence against women, it is important that schools make this issue a priority in the education of young people.
Madam President, the first thing I would like to do tonight is to thank Mr Scottà for his excellent willingness to negotiate this report.
It has been a real pleasure working with him and with the other spokespersons from the parliamentary groups.

We are proud of the position that will be demonstrated in the vote in this European Parliament tomorrow, and we are proud of a position such as this one, which defends quality, which promotes the development and enhancement of rural areas, not only as a

mere testimonies of a traditional European culture, but as real agents creating wealth and jobs, jobs which we so badly need in these times of economic crisis and dramatic employment levels in some Member States.
We also like the defence that this European Parliament maintains and the position that the Committee on Agriculture has already taken, which is clear: to go for quality to the maximum degree.
At this highest level, we certainly support maintaining the distinction between appellations of origin and protected geographical indications.
Of course, we reject the idea of lowering quality, and we want attention to be paid to and value placed on Community production.
I welcome the Commissioner's words.
It was a pleasure to listen to him talking about the commitment to the future of the European farming sector.
Of course, we hope that it listens to the voice of this Parliament, which will become clear tomorrow in that vote.

Finally, we hope that the European Commission never again gets ahead of Parliament by banning things such as marketing standards for fruit and vegetables, which have done so much damage to European free competition.
We want to correct the market imbalances and we also want to promote quality, variety, food security and an adequate level of production.
We also want Community agricultural production and the quality of that production to be defended in the World Trade Organisation.
We also have the work of Commissioner Cholovski to help us with that, because our producers and our European consumers cannot continue to be left defenceless.
Mr President, the Commissioner has spoken of the absurdity and obsolescence of the procedures.

for example, by monitoring passengers' rights and also by mediating or calling for a truce in the various collective conflicts in land transport,

As I was unable to do so on 11 March, International Day of the Victims of Terrorism, I would like to pay a well-deserved tribute to them from this House today, to recognise the enormous sacrifice of those who have paid the highest price in the name of freedom.

We urgently need to start work on a directive to protect the rights of victims of terrorism, who are always the great forgotten ones and who suffer the humiliation of being unjustifiably equated with their tormentors.

It is high time that these European institutions recognised the dignity of the victims of terrorism and helped them in their right to protection and justice, and that this is soon translated into a law

will honour all those who make it possible, and that the first article is that support for victims of terrorism should be to recognise the right to justice and that no government should ever pay a political price.

I call on all those who can defend the victims never to forget that true peace is born of justice, and that is the only peace that we in Europe and everywhere else in the world should defend if we believe in the dignity of the human being.

Mr President, it has been necessary to find ourselves facing the abyss and economic collapse for Heads of State and Government to understand that the way forward is not more nationalism, but more Europe.
Measures adopted in this direction include the erosion of the right of veto in the European Council or the seed of a European public treasury.
Finally, albeit belatedly, we have realised that we are all in the same boat and that it is pointless to deal with individual situations, particularly when doing so in an improvised manner.

But I wonder whether this attitude is a parenthesis or, on the contrary, the beginning of a real European economic government, because what we have in abundance, ladies and gentlemen, is governance and what we lack is real government, if we want to remain faithful to the legacy of the founding fathers.
That is why we in Parliament must promote all those reforms aimed at strengthening Europe and making budgetary and fiscal discipline a reality, forgetting, by the way, about nostalgic sovereignties.
Ladies and gentlemen, strengthening the European Commission and strengthening this Parliament is the only correct recipe for building a Europe that, otherwise, would fade away like a fleeting shadow.
Mr President, Commissioner, on behalf of the Committee on International Trade, I would like to thank Mrs De Sarnez for the cooperation we have enjoyed.
Today more than ever, the Union for the Mediterranean needs to strengthen its major projects and institutional development.
Today more than ever, we need to find solutions to get out of the crisis, to research, to climate change, to water, to new energy models and to sustainable development.

To this end, it is necessary to promote and consolidate trade and economic relations in the region, with short- and long-term measures, such as a free trade area, the removal of tariff barriers and the opening up of North-South and South-South markets.

The Barcelona Summit, the Spanish Presidency, is going to provide the opportunity for a new commitment, a new situation, a new joint effort to achieve democratisation, economic solutions and social solutions.

Madam President, among the trans-European transport corridors there is a railway corridor called the Mediterranean Corridor.
It is a vital communication link that will connect North Africa with Europe.
For Spain, this is vital, because this axis is a priority, given that it would link Algeciras with Europe.
Without excluding other priority corridors, it would pass through Almería, Murcia, the Valencian Community and Catalonia.
The reason, as Commissioner Reding said, is greater economic activity and a high concentration of road transport.
This is one of the two conditions.
It would open up the possibility of communicating with different ports in the Mediterranean area.
We need to gain economic momentum and be competitive – as the Commissioner said – because more than 50% of Spanish exports are produced in this region.
Therefore, if we increase the issue of a strategic rail axis along the Mediterranean, we will increase competitiveness for Spain and competitiveness for Europe.

common position on Cuba and to create a new type of relationship between the European Union and Cuba, which naturally includes the defence of human rights as a priority.
That policy has not been crowned with success, basically because of the intransigence of the Cuban dictatorship, which began during this Presidency in January of

this year, with the expulsion of the Member speaking to you, when he was trying to visit the island, with all the formalities and in full compliance with the law, in order to express concern about human rights there.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, this is a regulation of the utmost importance for consumers and for the food industry, because it is not easy at all to achieve balanced labelling that contains all the essential information but is not so exhaustive as to complicate the information.
This is a great report and I should like to congratulate the rapporteur on it.
I agree with her on the main issues relating to nutritional information and the controversial issue of profiles, especially if we take into account that the Commission did not comply with the
commitment to establish these profiles and their conditions of use before 19 January 2009, as required by Regulation (EC) No 1924 / 2006.

One issue that concerns me is the possibility of national labelling rules coexisting, because this goes against the objective of the legislation, which is harmonisation and avoiding all possible obstacles to the proper functioning of a genuine single market.
As regards the indication of the country of origin, current legislation provides that this information must be given when failure to indicate it could mislead the consumer.
In addition, voluntary labelling is planned.
Going further could involve economic costs and administrative complexity for businesses without providing any clear, significant benefit to consumers.
As for the use of different languages, I have to say that the Commission proposal is in line with the existing directive, which has worked well and has not raised any problems.
Reopening this debate could be dangerous and unnecessary and could cause problems for the circulation of products.
Mr President, the best proof of the goodwill we have is the thousand amendments we are seeing from the committee to the plenary.

However, in addition to good intentions, I think we need to be practical when it comes to labelling food, if we do not want to achieve the opposite of what we are aiming for, that is, confusing the consumer instead of helping him or her to become informed.
It is not a question of illustrating, but of preventing, of informing them about what they can eat and how much.
We need to label on fats, on sugars, on trans fats.
Indeed.
Indeed, on those things that affect cholesterol, on all those substances that affect what we have said, obesity, the health of people.

Yes, but for some other foods, with some other information, such as place of origin, when it does not lead to confusion, or, for example, in the case of meat, stating the place of birth, the place of transit, the place of grazing and the place where the animal was slaughtered may be very illustrative, but it will end up causing confusion.

It is up to us to be practical and ensure that this regulation is the one that harmonises and informs consumers, or, on the contrary, the one that confuses them even more and imposes issues that, rather than achieving better health outcomes, will achieve greater confusion.

For that reason, I ask that we opt for the Guideline Daily Amount (GDA) instead of traffic lights, for example, because it is very clear that if you consume this amount or twice that amount, you will get fat, so consume only one.
Daily intake levels, as opposed to other types of codes, is what some of us are proposing, so as not to confuse consumers any further.
Madam President, thank you very much, Mr Fraguero, for being here on behalf of the Spanish Presidency in a report such as this, Commissioner.
Mr Milana, congratulations on your ability to reach a common agreement, to bring together all the interests and to put on the table an own-initiative report, one of the first own-initiative reports.
The development of aquaculture is important.

We need to do this because fish consumption is increasing and because fishing extraction capacity has stagnated. However, I would like to stress the economic importance of this activity, which generates wealth and employment.
Health guarantees are important, sustainability is important and protecting the marine environment is important.
We need to improve the image of aquaculture and we also need to promote the scientific and research sector.
Aquaculture is an opportunity, as Mr Fraguiero said, within the CFP, as the Commissioner said.

We need to give it a consistent framework for coordination and we need to give it the possibility of becoming regular, because aquaculture can provide self-sufficiency, sustainability and economic resources, and I think that is important for Europe.

Mr President, in my opinion, this excellent report outlines the main guidelines that should inspire the new White Paper on transport, which should shape European transport policy for the next ten years.

This report contains several points worthy of mention: the need for a financial fund as an essential instrument; the challenge of the growing demand for freight transport; the search for multimodal solutions that

security for users and goods; the pressing need for sufficient and safe rest areas in road transport; and the diagnosis, in my opinion, that there should not be mere competition between the different modes

modes, but rather to complement each other in pursuit of efficiency criteria. The rapporteur's statement, which I consider to be both courageous and fair, that rail or maritime transport is not always more environmentally friendly than road transport.
In short, the concept of an efficient community is a concept for the future and, in my opinion, should undoubtedly be supported with all due rigour.
yours, Mr President, is a mistake in approach.
You thought you were really going to preside over Europe and influence the gears that make the complex European machine work.
He even came to believe, because that is what people close to you let him proclaim, that the world was going to experience a stellar moment under your Presidency.
None of this has been made reality.
Let us make some clarifications.

In these six months of the Spanish Presidency, I would like to distinguish, on the one hand, between the political direction of the Presidency and, on the other, the work of the officials who have been working in the engine rooms.
It is the first one to have failed, and this is due to the absence of adequate impetus from the Presidency of the Government, which has instead been driven by the pendulum.
However, I would like to highlight the efforts of the officials and their valuable willingness to serve Europe and Spain.
Mr President, Spain has magnificent professional civil servants and if there is one thing to regret, it is that they are too marginalised in everyday reality.
That said, it is right to acknowledge that some commendable initiatives have been adopted in these six months.
Among them, I am pleased to mention the organ donation and transplant plan and the treatment given to patients ’ rights in cross-border healthcare.
The agreements on the new 2020 strategy and, above all, those on the European External Action Service are still too fragile.
The outcome of the European protection order for victims of gender violence has been regrettable, and the same gloomy result has been offered by the
basic Community patent, vital for the lives of thousands of companies, is partly blocked by the linguistic problems that Spanish legislation encourages.

But all the major political initiatives have been spectacular failures, from the Mediterranean Summit to the EU-US Summit, which he cites, and for which his own Government was responsible for creating unrealistic expectations.
However, we must agree that not being able to move forward with its policy on Cuba has been highly positive for the Cubans.
Finally, the progress made on the road to better European economic governance cannot in any way be credited to your Presidency, which was conspicuously absent on the most important issues.
Goodbye, then, to the Spanish Presidency.

I would like to draw one positive conclusion from this experience: as a committed European, I am excited by the thought that it could mean the end of the rotating presidencies, as they have been conceived until now.

This would be good news, because as long as Europe does not strengthen its common institutions by solidly uniting its fragments, it will continue to run the risk of being submerged in the empty spaces of irrelevance.
Mr President, believe me, Mr Zapatero, I would have liked to make a different speech today.
She presented a Semester full of ambition and set her sights so high that, when compared with what was actually achieved, disappointment is inevitable.
The truth is, ladies and gentlemen, that it has not been able to overcome a complex situation for which leadership and credibility were needed.
You have said on occasion that the European Union is a club with rules, and I agree.
But it was you, Madam President, who should have demanded that those rules be complied with.
The reality is that it was the Spanish Government that was being urged to meet its obligations.
In this context, the positive achievements, of which there has been no shortage, have been overshadowed.
You have had time to relate them; I have not.
Democracy would be nothing more than a public opinion regime.
And it seems that this is the case: Obama ’ s disdain, the cancellation of the Mediterranean summit and the announced EU-US summit are serious failures.
The rush to close improvised agreements at any price has not helped the image of Spanish management, which has been described as a 'fixer' in its way of achieving some respectable objective.
Spain is a great nation and will continue to be so despite the poor management of this period.
Our diplomats and officials have kept their heads, and thanks to that, Spain ’ s prestige will continue.
The truth is that the assessment of this Presidency will depend more on the situation in Spain than on what has or has not been done here.
It would be difficult for someone who has plunged his country into a period of uncertainty and anxiety to lead such a difficult company.
At best, this Presidency has been sadly irrelevant.
Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, in the coming months we will be fully involved in the debate on the new CAP.

It is important to reach a good agreement, so I hope that the Belgian Presidency will achieve what the Spanish Presidency failed to achieve, namely to steer the discussions in such a way that the Member States commit themselves to maintaining a policy in the future

common agricultural policy that is truly strong, with a sufficient budget to meet the needs of the 27 Member States, both the new and the old, and so that it does not happen again as in 2005, when there was not enough money and modulation had to be invented.

In the next CAP reform, we are gambling on the viability of a sector that not only provides intangible goods and services, such as caring for the environment, but also should ensure that European citizens are supplied with food.

We have previously witnessed painful situations of scarcity, so the European Parliament needs to provide security and supplies based on Community production as the main objective of the next reform, without neglecting other contributions such as the fight against climate change.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I agree with Commissioner Cioloş that this report is necessary, very necessary, and comes at a good time, because the abuses committed by large-scale distribution have been demonstrated, severely upsetting the various links in the food chain.

The Bové report, which at times may go too far in trying to be too interventionist, must be acknowledged, from the point of view of the social market economy that we advocate in the Group of the European People's Party (Christian Democrats), as gathering responses to the legitimate demands of European producers.

It also brings transparency to the different parts of the food chain, which is always a good thing, and gives the weaker sectors of that chain many more opportunities and support to defend themselves, and, of course, supports European consumers so that they know more about that chain.

The European food industry could have been taken more into account in this report; perhaps that would have been very good. However, this report also includes measures to curb price volatility, and that was very necessary.

It also includes measures to ensure fairness for those links in the chain, as requested by the Committee on the Environment, and also calls for action to combat the abusive and unfair practices of large-scale distribution.
It is high time that we established a list of abusive practices and that European legislation banned them outright.
It is also important that measures are taken to prevent unfair practices through 'private label' products, which go against competition and against intellectual property law.

For all these reasons, we must support the Bové report and all the Members from the different parliamentary committees – the Committee on Industry, External Trade, the Committee on the Environment, Public Health and Food Safety and the Committee on Agriculture and Rural Development – who have worked hard to bring this report to light and help European producers move forward.
The alarm bell is ringing, and it is true.
There is also frustration, as we have been hearing in the speeches in this plenary, at not having achieved the Millennium Development Goals.
The truth is that a lot is at stake.

Our natural environment is so fragile that if one day the big fish decided that it was not going to continue eating the small fish, we would all have to pay for it out of our pockets, with our health and our welfare as the currency.
The European Union is currently a leader in the fight against climate change and has been for some time, thanks to its pioneering and conservation measures.

However, as the unmet objectives show, it seems that there has been a lack of will, not only to implement and coordinate, which has also been mentioned here, but also to expand, to go beyond our very limited special conservation zones.
Biodiversity is certainly not a museum piece that can be locked away in a ghetto, as sometimes seems to happen, and visited from time to time.
Obesity is alive and has no borders: it crosses the whole world like a domino effect, in which if one piece falls, all the others fall.

I would like to draw attention to the need for a more global and community-wide approach, especially with regard to land, because subsidiarity as the only method for managing land or forests could be one of the new terrible enemies of biodiversity.

That is why I draw attention to paragraph 67 and the possible vote that will be held, and I ask you to reject the second part, because I am convinced that a common policy on soils would help to preserve the entire territory of the Union.

European soil, so varied, so diverse, for better or for worse, and so lacking in political will on the part of the Member States, and so in need of ever more European Union, with all the meaning that word has, more union also for the ground.

Mr President, Commissioner, first of all, I would like to highlight the work of the rapporteur, Mrs McAvan, not only in terms of content, but also in terms of form, because she has done it in collaboration with all the shadow rapporteurs.
I would also like to thank the experts from the Spanish administration for their contribution to this agreement.

The purpose of this European Union pharmacovigilance system is to strengthen the monitoring, transparency and communication of the safety of medicines once they are on the market, especially with regard to their risk profiles and any adverse reactions they may cause.

The creation of the European Pharmacovigilance Committee should be highlighted. It will assess and issue recommendations to the European Medicines Agency on any safety problems that may arise with medicines marketed in the European Union.

The solution reached for the notification of suspected adverse reactions to the pharmacovigilance system is very important, both from the Member States and from those responsible for marketing the medicines.

EudraVigilance will be the only source of information, but at the same time the competent authority in the Member State where the suspected adverse reaction has taken place will be informed, and this is very important.
We should also highlight the possibility opened up for consumers to report any adverse reactions, and in this respect, the creation and management by the Agency of a portal
European Medicines Agency on medicines safety, which in turn links to the portals of each Member State, will help to improve citizens ’ knowledge of the safety of the medicines they consume.
Finally, we cannot forget that, as pharmacovigilance plays a fundamental role in protecting public health, the adoption of the directive and the
This directive and regulation will make a significant contribution to this end by improving not only current procedures but also the coordination of actions between the different Member States.

I appreciate the rapporteur's efforts in accepting some suggestions and, of course, her defence of copyright, which I support. However, the inclusion of the infringing and sanctionable nature of certain exchanges and the

recognition of sanctioning authorities and suppliers, as well as in the alternative resolution, the exclusion of mass exchanges as a possible infringement, make it impossible to vote in favour of either of the two reports by Ms Liza.
The Gallo report has aroused great interest and could have been timely, but the substance of the debate is too much focused on the debate in favour of or against the French law known as the '
PI, of cutting off internet access for offenders, without there having been a genuine European debate that takes into account the reality of other progress in national legislation.

I hope that we achieve a common position in the Socialist Group and a majority in the European Parliament on the debates and amendments on the Commission's draft directive, which will soon reach Parliament, according to Commissioner Barnier's announcement.
On that occasion, we will try to reach a more balanced position than today, one which takes into account both the rights of internet users and the essential need to respect intellectual property.
I congratulate those who have worked on this report, on this financial package, especially the officials and the Members of this Parliament.
A financial crisis of enormous proportions has had to occur for Europe to wake up.
However, an opportunity has been lost, in part, to strengthen Europe ’ s common institutions, because, on the one hand, these new supervisory authorities have a scope of competence
limited compared to similar authorities in Member States, and it is a pity that no progress has been made towards an integration similar to that which took place with the system of central banks.
We have made progress, but we need to keep climbing the mountain.

As a member of the Human Rights Committee, as a Spanish and European citizen and as a victim of terrorism since the terrorist organisation ETA murdered my brother and his wife in Seville, I am addressing this Parliament to denounce the Government of Venezuela and its President for having allowed terrorists from

in Venezuelan soil under the command of the alleged terrorist Cubilla, who works for the government of Hugo Chávez, despite the fact that there is an international arrest warrant against him, which constitutes a serious violation of the fundamental right to security of any European citizen.
I demand that the Spanish Government take strong action in the face of this serious humiliation of all Spaniards.
Zapatero cannot continue to remain silent while the Venezuelan ambassador calls our Civil Guards torturers, who have been and will be an example of bravery in the fight against ETA.
nor can it look the other way in the face of this growing cooperation between ETA and the FARC on Venezuelan soil, as Judge Eloy Velasco has already denounced.
Zapatero cannot shake the hand of a leader like Chávez, who is turning Venezuela into a refuge for terrorists.

The President of a country like Spain, where terrorism has caused so much pain, cannot continue to ignore this problem and should give the response that it deserves and that any ordinary citizen would give if they had the chance.
On 14 February 2008, as a Valentine ’ s Day present, the Conference of Presidents decided that the intergroups should only meet on Thursdays.
Since then, we have tried to resolve this issue.
We have not achieved it.
Mr President, the intergroups now have to meet on a date that is not appropriate.
They almost never have rooms and they never have interpretation.

Today I am speaking not only as Chair of the Intergroup on Poverty and Human Rights, but also with the mandate of the majority of the intergroups in this House, and therefore of the Members of this House who are part of those intergroups.
I solemnly request that the Conference of Presidents reconsider and annul the decision of 14 February 2008 and return to the previous situation of freedom of intergroups.
If you do not do so, I also say kindly that we will bring the matter to the plenary of this House and it will decide who is sovereign in this House, which is this Parliament.
Mr President, on the International Day against Trafficking in Persons and Exploitation, some figures were revealed that should make us act like reptiles.
Over 90% of prostitution in Europe is based on blackmail and extortion.

In the light of these figures, we should ask ourselves whether we are doing everything necessary to protect the dignity and integrity of thousands of women whose rights are also protected by the Charter of Fundamental Rights, which we adopted with the Treaty of Lisbon.
Because we are not talking about the oldest trade in the world, but about the only form of slavery that we have not managed to eradicate from old Europe.
Countries such as Spain are making significant efforts in this respect, by pursuing traffickers, raising awareness among citizens and promoting reception plans for women who are victims.
But it is not enough.
Europe must be a single area of action and commitment.

The next Tackling Directive is an opportunity to act on demand through education and to make it more difficult to access, for example, the offer to advertise in all types of media, as well as increasing bilateral cooperation with third countries to combat this scourge at source.
We have an opportunity.
I call on Parliament to continue to be at the forefront of citizens ’ rights.
Madam President, we all agree that the External Action Service is a historic opportunity for European integration.

However, in order to do so, we need to act with vision, because now is not the time for internal squabbles over resources that are passed on or withheld in the short term, or for wars between the various European institutions.
What is required now, on the contrary, is to have a general vision of what the European Union should be in the world, in other words, to do the exact opposite of what
what happened a few days ago on the occasion of the award of the Nobel Peace Prize, when we heard too many voices from Europe, and not all of them brave.
I would like to highlight one of the exceptions: the President of this Parliament, Mr Buzek, has issued a brilliant and dignified statement.

Mr President, the regulation we are debating today is of vital importance for the future of a sector, cutlery and related products, which in my region, the land of Don Quixote, and specifically in Albacete, represents not only the survival of more than 8 000 jobs, but also an element of identity and a commitment to the future of craftsmanship as a source of employment.
The Meidín seal on knives has long been claimed by social groups such as Aprecu and contested by the Government of Castilla-La Mancha with the unanimous support of its regional parliament.
origin marking is not interventionism; it is a commitment to transparency, to fair trading rules and to the fight against unfair competition.

It is to increase consumers ’ decision-making capacity when choosing a product, so that they take into account not only the final price, but also the quality mark, the place of origin or the social conditions under which they are produced.

Above all, it will represent a new horizon of optimism for many people who are having a hard time but who today look to us thinking that we have not been indifferent when it comes to promoting fairer trade and also adding value to those who, in addition to producing, do so respecting social rights and the best conditions for European workers.
For that reason, I thank the rapporteur and Mr Menéndez del Valle for their political support and their social awareness on this important issue for my country.
Mr President, let me try to bring a little calm to this lively debate.
It is regrettable that some of the countries or some of the states that make up the European Union continue to be present in this G20 forum.
Despite this anomaly, which certainly weakens Europe ’ s image in the world, it would be good if at least a global common position existed in that global forum.
And what would that common position be, in my opinion?

In my humble opinion, I believe that firstly, the adoption of a global agreement to deal with the consequences of the crisis cannot be delayed, and not just a financial agreement. Secondly, Europe must maintain the euro as a reference currency, or, if you prefer, as an anchor for

avoid letting market turbulence drag us down and make us repeat the mistakes made throughout the twentieth century. The euro, thirdly, must serve as a herald of what Europe should represent in the world in terms of democratic values and freedoms.
public.
In short, we need to know that in the global world, in this global game, those who only play national cards lose.
Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, I think we should congratulate the rapporteur, Mrs Meissner.
there were different, opposing positions, and she has obtained compromise solutions that allow us all to be together on an important issue.
Ladies and gentlemen, the Committee on Transport and Tourism has decided that all ports should be protected in a similar way, and it argues that otherwise there would be unfair competition.

We accept the majority position in favour of a legal situation with uniform rules for the whole of the Union, but I would not fail, ladies and gentlemen, to point out clearly that this also involves a certain lack of fairness.
It is clear that the Baltic Sea, the North Sea and the English Channel are more polluted areas, and that the controls on the sulphur limit values should be stricter in these cases.
We should positively assess the measures relating to ports, as they affect solutions for reducing transport times and handling costs.
In any case, I will never tire of repeating that we have a pending signature on this point.
Without a decisive liberalisation of ports, they will not be able to expand the central role they must play in the context of transport.
Ladies and gentlemen, I also welcome the drive towards a strategy aimed at modifying the specific repercussions of climate change in
I think this is a positive report that we should vote for.
However, we cannot forget that this is taking place against the backdrop of a serious disaster, that of the Gulf of Mexico.

In this respect, the Commission is called upon to assess whether the review of the European Maritime Safety Agency regulation strengthens the role that the Agency should play in inspections for prevention and in post-cleaning tasks following oil spills.
My personal opinion, Commissioner, is that they should be reinforced with the mandate that is required for that purpose.
Mr President, integrated maritime policy is a cross-sectoral, regional and sectoral policy. We must include fisheries and aquaculture policy, and we must not subordinate this to other policies.
We must all respond to environmental and economic problems, both for society in general and for the fishing sector. New technologies, fishing gear, species development, a maritime economy plan.

to create more and better jobs, quality supplies, international relations that prevent piracy and to promote the governance of our seas, which do not have borders and do not have sustainability problems, as in the case of Iceland.
This would be working towards an integrated maritime policy. This would be working towards the environment and sustainability.
Mr President, recently, the Church of Our Lady of Perpetual Help in Baghdad was attacked and 58 people were murdered, most of them women and children, simply because they were Christians.
Al-Qaeda has proudly claimed responsibility for the feat.
This is not an isolated incident, but the culmination of the persecution and attacks on Christians in Muslim-majority countries.
Expulsions in Morocco, threats in Turkey, attacks in India and Nigeria.
In January, six Christians were massacred at the door of a church in Cairo.
How long will the complicit silence of Western democracies continue?
Fortunately, President Buzek has made an institutional statement.
The statement of condemnation by the High Representative, Mrs Ashton, was limited to condemning a terrorist act.
terrorism. Why is the motive not mentioned: the fact that they were Christians, which is the unequivocal cause of the massacre?
I have heard nothing from Mr Zapatero, nothing from President Erdoğan.
Mr Erdoğan, what is the Alliance of Civilisations for?
I am sorry for our fanaticism and our cowardice.

Alessandra, the much lamented and misunderstood Oriana Fallaci, who announced the arrival of Eurabia and was almost stoned to death for having the courage to denounce what is happening in complex Europe.
I also agree with the desire of this House to have a clear, transparent and effective system for the allocation of the budget.
I want the Europeans' proposals to receive the support they deserve in Cancún.
With regard to this debate, we must stress the importance of cities in preserving the environment.
There is a pact between more than a thousand mayors in the European Union, which demonstrates that these authorities recognise the problem, and their action will be decisive if we want to succeed.
All the fine words in the world are useless if we do not have the collaboration of city mayors.
To this end, it is very important that emissions are made public, as cities produce almost 80% of greenhouse gases.
Today, there is no such disclosure, and, worse still, there is no common standard for measuring these emissions themselves.

There is a non-profit organisation called the Carbon Dioxide Information Analysis Centre, which already holds information on thousands of companies and which I think could also be used by local councils.
Without their involvement, without the involvement of these local councils and mayors, environmental action will come to nothing, will be smoke, the very smoke we want to escape from.

Mr President, some people have said that the condemnation of Morocco is too mild, given the scale of the human rights violations in the Sahara, where Morocco is nothing more than an occupying country with no sovereignty and no legal title whatsoever.
But the important thing is the message that the whole of Europe and this Parliament can no longer tolerate their violence.
Yesterday, I was talking about the Hague Tribunal in this very Parliament.

The international community, which sadly failed to prevent the genocide in Rwanda, Yugoslavia and Darfur, I hope will prevent the one in Western Sahara, and we do not have to see it before another court, also in The Hague, as he said, but this time for war crimes.
Mr President, Commissioner, the directive adopted in 2009 was a success because it updated the legislative framework that has been regulating safety in toys.
It is a big step in the right direction, because it shows us the way forward, but it is not enough.
We must always be vigilant and watchful that the toys that are marketed do not endanger children ’ s safety and health.
All toys on the market have to comply with current legislation.
We must revise and improve surveillance mechanisms to detect dangerous toys, whether due to substances considered toxic, CMRs, fragrances and other dangerous substances such as HPA compounds.
The rules to be followed need to be clearer on warnings regarding the minimum age and the dangers of inappropriate use.
The recommended age for toys must be based on studies of children ’ s behaviour.
We must be able to adapt our legislation to scientific advances based on sound studies and be stricter in monitoring the market and the import of toys.
We need to work together and strengthen cooperation between Member States and to have joint work by customs to control the route of imported toys.
Citizens are preparing to do their Christmas shopping and we must give them the assurance that the products they buy meet all safety requirements.
In this debate, we have seen clearly that we all agree on supporting the objective of this report.
The various political groups have shown their support for this directive, which offers greater protection for victims.
Therefore, I ask myself: what is preventing us from adopting this instrument?
I repeat: what fears are there that something as necessary and simple as protecting victims equally throughout Europe should finally become a reality?
Hoping that this Parliament will approve this report by a large majority, I turn to the Council and the Commission to remind them that we have
legal obstacles in our negotiations, and I welcome the fact that the Commission recognises our work, just as we will recognise the work it does in the future.

We should not exclude each other, but help each other, by approving this more specific instrument today and, tomorrow, the broader package of measures to assist victims that the Commission will present, and with which I pledge to cooperate.
Therefore, let us today be brave and responsible and help all those who have the right to live in a safer Europe.
Let us help everyone, I repeat, whether men, women or children.
Let us help those who are now looking to us to give them a better life, a life that gives back their freedom, their security and, above all, their dignity.

Madam President, the Group of the European People ' s Party (Christian Democrats) will vote enthusiastically in favour of this report, and my congratulations first to the rapporteurs, the Commission and the Council for this agreement.
Ladies and gentlemen, in the 1980s, we coined the term ‘ democratic deficit ’.
It was intended to mean that in the European Union, decisions were taken solely by the Council of Ministers, on the Commission ’ s proposals, and that Parliament was consulted only.
Since the 1980s, the battle of this Parliament, through the Treaty of Maastricht, Amsterdam, Nice, the Constitutional Treaty and the Treaty of Lisbon, has been

to close that democratic deficit. I believe that with the Treaty of Lisbon, with the new powers of the European Parliament and with the participation of the national parliaments, we have achieved that objective.
We are rounding it off today with this Citizens ’ Initiative, which was due in the Convention's work to the good cooperation between national parliaments and the European Parliament.
I would like to mention the names of Mr Mayer, our German Social Democrat colleague, and Mr Lamassoure, who brought that initiative to the Convention and ensured that it was adopted.
Therefore, I believe that we have come full circle, Madam President.
I think that when we vote on the initiative today, we will have put the death certificate of the concept of democratic deficit on the table.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is now our turn to act responsibly, and today we also have the opportunity to do so.
By voting on the Union budget, we will fulfil what is expected of us: a responsible Parliament and a co-decision Parliament.
I think it is a good day for Europe, ladies and gentlemen.
Madam President, in January 2012, 400 Spanish egg producers could disappear, around 30% of the total in my country, with the loss of a production of 300 000 tonnes of eggs.

The European Union could stop producing those 80 million eggs, those two million tonnes, and we would only end up with imports from third countries whose animal welfare standards are much lower than those of the European Union, if we do not act intelligently.
This 1999 directive, which obliges us to increase the space in laying hen cages, requires us to act intelligently and with a positive vision, because otherwise,
we will only weaken European production and give extra trade opportunities to third countries, where the space for birds is infinitely worse than the current situation in the European Union.

This is a directive that requires a great deal of effort from European producers. In Spain alone, we understand that the cost will increase by around EUR 600 million, but we must not forget that it has an economic impact that extends to the livestock industry and the food industry in general.

I therefore ask for your support for the amendment tabled by the Group of the European People's Party (Christian Democrats) to the second paragraph, where we ask for a solution to be found at least for responsible farms, for responsible farmers who are transforming their farms and who will be almost finished by January 2012.

We must support them in transforming these facilities and give them time to complete this process, thus avoiding irreparable damage to farms and preventing a sudden situation of scarcity in the Community market, consequently increasing prices for consumers.
We need to respect the directive, give the egg and egg product industry a chance, and at the same time respect animal welfare and consumers' right to reasonable prices.

Mr President, I think it is only fair to thank the rapporteurs, especially Mrs Grossetête and Mrs Rühle from my own group, for the work they have done to get a regulation out of a deadlock and make it useful, because their work has allowed public health to be included as a legal basis

and to have a new direction that allows us to lay the foundations that must guarantee our citizens the security and quality that they choose at any given time. Above all, it also allows us to clearly regulate the role of the Member States, both those of origin, which have to make the payment, and those in which treatment is received.

However, I do not think we should be satisfied with that alone, but rather that this should be the beginning of a much greater advance that, as Mrs Rühle said, makes this directive a mere formality, because the level of equality achieved in all the Member States makes it unnecessary for citizens to move to each country.

In that struggle, we must obtain a standard that guarantees citizens more rights in relation to the care they have to receive and benefits and security, rather than the mere right they already have to mobility between Member States.
Equality is what will make the Union work, otherwise we will be taking very small steps.
Madam President, the Spanish Socialist delegation voted in favour, as did the rest of our group, but I would not leave here without making it clear that I am concerned that this directive includes
the issue of organ transplants, against the opinion of the Committee on the Environment, which was the result of the Council ’ s constant insistence on including transplants in this directive.

I hope that this inclusion will give full effect to the good legislation that we adopted in this very Chamber, both the Transplantation Directive and the Action Plan, not so long ago, with the full support of the vast majority.

I warn that we will be vigilant to ensure that this inclusion does not undermine the good work done by successful organisations such as the Spanish National Transplant Organisation and, of course, our ambition to save 20 000 lives, which we had in the Transplantation Directive and in the Action Plan that, I repeat, we adopted here.
Therefore, I would like to make it clear that we will keep a close eye on the implementation of this directive in this respect.
Mr President, today is also European Day against Sexual Health.
Ladies and gentlemen, the figures are worrying: the World Health Organisation estimates that more than 300 million people contract a sexually transmitted disease each year, involuntarily.

In Europe, as the European Sexual Health Forum warns, the number of unwanted pregnancies among teenagers is worrying, due to excessive shortcomings in education and information among our young people.

Indeed, as the Commission points out, there are different and diverse inequalities in Europe in access to sexual health services, depending on the Member States and, within them, depending on citizens ’ income levels.
To combat all this, we need a European strategy.

Only in five Member States are there plans in this respect, but in all of them, the number of unwanted pregnancies, cases of sexually transmitted diseases and the number of abortions have been reduced.
a common strategy that promotes prevention through education, that expands information policies, that increases efforts in family planning and that facilitates access to contraceptives.
a cross-cutting and ambitious strategy, free from taboos, to guarantee all citizens' rights to sexual health in the safest and healthiest conditions possible.

Mr President, at a time of strong social tensions, one cannot but welcome with enthusiasm the great social agreement in Spain between the Government, the trade unions and the employers' organisations.
This agreement is exemplary for Europe, where we have not seen such successes for some time.

We should also take into account the arguments of Cándido Méndez, one of the main protagonists of that Spanish pact, who attended the meeting of the Economic and Social Committee in Brussels last Tuesday, 8 February.
Instead, he stressed the need to extend social dialogue to a European level and the need to have the voice and the initiatives of trade unions in our Europe.
So it must be.

Mr President, with all due respect to the issues of terrorism and immigration, I have noted that the European Union is unfortunately moving forward only in response to emergency situations, such as the serious

attack or a massive influx of immigrants, such as the one currently taking place in Lampedusa, where the exodus of citizens from Tunisia towards the Italian coast could end in a chronic situation of announced deaths.

The journey of hope could end up as a cursed journey, and it will weigh on our consciences, on the consciences of all those of us who witness these continuous, uncontrolled flows and do not say 'enough' with sufficient authority.
When it happens, we will be shocked, but the following day we will forget about it.

European citizens have the right and the duty to know the truth: that the Mediterranean strategy is not working, that the common immigration policy will remain a dream as long as European countries do not commit themselves to providing sufficient financial resources to make Frontex effective.
Talking costs nothing, but we have to act.
Do you really believe that Frontex can guarantee the security of Europe ’ s borders today?
I do not think so.

Let us hope that the citizens of Tunisia, Egypt and other Maghreb countries do not decide to look for a better future in Europe, but rather in their own countries, because that is what they have fought for, and we should contribute to helping them.
Therefore, solidarity, responsibility, but above all, truth, when it comes to talking about a common European immigration policy, which is more necessary than ever today.

Madam President, it has already been said that the backbone of pension systems in Europe is and must remain the public system, because it is the only system that guarantees cohesion and the only one that also reduces the risk of poverty in our society.

It is still alarming that in OECD countries, the greatest inequalities in citizens ' income are found in the area of pensions, or that, due to the crisis, private pensions lost 20% of their value in 2008 alone and are still far from the solvency levels required today.

All this shows that, at a time of unavoidable reforms, the sustainability of our pension systems must be addressed in a cross-cutting manner, taking into account, among other factors, ambitious policies on birth rates or work-life balance.

ambitious policies for access to and permanence in employment, or even immigration policies based on integration, which will also guarantee, from a demographic point of view, the sustainability of our pensions.
Finally, I regret that the text does not place limits on the abuse of traineeships in companies, since, as a general rule, these people are excluded from all kinds of social security contributions.

In my opinion, this is essential for an entire new generation of workers, who, if they are being asked to do more in order to have a decent pension, should also be able to do less in order to access decent work.

Mr President, Commissioner, we are at a key moment in the development of the future cohesion policy, which, following the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, focuses not only on economic and social aspects, but also on the territorial aspect.
Territorial cohesion aims to achieve harmonious development between territories so that their citizens can make the most of their territorial particularities.
However, in order to achieve this objective, we also need to make progress in the area of cooperation between regions of the Union that face the same challenges and problems.

This is the objective pursued by the European Union strategy for the Atlantic region: a common perspective to address the common challenges faced by the States in the region, including marine research, maritime surveillance and environmental and economic challenges.

In order to achieve our objectives, we need to adopt an integrated strategic approach that is in line with the Europe 2020 strategy and the European Union's policies for the period after 2013, especially regional policy and maritime policy.
However, ladies and gentlemen, above all, this strategy must be based on renewed multi-level governance, based on closer participation by public authorities

regional and local authorities, the Member States, the European Union, and the stakeholders from the private sector and civil society organisations, including interregional organisations and networks.

It is a very significant day to talk about equality between men and women, because we are celebrating International Women ’ s Day, remembering those who, 100 years ago, fought for labour and economic rights and for women ’ s suffrage.
We have come a long way in the past 100 years, but there is still a lot to be done, and true equality is still a dream, while discrimination against women is a reality.

I believe that each one of us, men and women, can do much more to achieve the true emancipation of women, because there are many women in the world whose right to life is threatened, their dignity violated and their freedom denied.
Allow me, on this International Women ’ s Day, to express my support and hope for all the women fighting for their democratic rights in the revolutions in North Africa.
Because a woman cannot be worth half of a man, neither in Arab countries nor anywhere else in the world.
Therefore, we are worth the same and deserve the same.
I will continue fighting to achieve it and, until that equality exists, Women ' s Day will continue to exist.
Although many people do not want to see it, it is a fact that women earn half of what men do, and that domestic violence affects women and children in particular.
I repeat: we must continue to denounce all these events in Parliament and, of course, I promise that I will continue working.
Mr President, I would like to express my agreement with the opinion of the Members who have spoken here this morning and who are insisting on respect for Parliament and for the Commission.
Therefore, my modest affirmative vote will depend on whether the European Council is prepared to admit the role of the institutions mentioned.
because the new mechanism we are discussing must be an important component of the economic governance conducted by a European Union
capable of ensuring consistency between the common economic policy, which must set clear, precise and limited objectives, and this stability instrument.

In other words, we must do everything we can to prevent this effort that we have embarked upon from ending up on the intergovernmental track, which is a dead end, or on the track of the dominance of a few states, because all of this would frustrate reform.
Ladies and gentlemen, Europe is built stone by stone, and this is one of the stones that strengthens and balances this delicate structure.
There are essential points to improve, so we must send the report back to committee.
Thus, the parliamentary journey of this report does not end here.
The initial proposal was bad.
That is what consumer associations told us, both at national level and at European level.
and I particularly value the work of the Asturias Consumers Union and its tireless president, Dace Alonso.
We have managed to have the general principle of maximum harmonisation of the old Article 4 rejected.

We must say very clearly that maximum harmonisation, establishing the same protection in all Member States, would mean a lowering of protection for many consumers, as there is a greater tradition of protecting consumers' rights in various countries.

In any case, I would like to stress that it is essential to ensure that the list of unfair contractual terms is not exhaustive, so that each Member State can increase the number of terms it considers unacceptable and invalid in contracts with consumers.

to reverse the burden of proof in individual clauses, so that traders have to prove that clauses that deviate from the standard have been individually negotiated, and that contractual clauses must be expressed in a clear and comprehensible manner.
We continue to oppose maximum harmonisation in relation to unfair terms.
According to Eurobarometer, 79% of traders believe that full harmonisation in this area will have little or no effect on their activities abroad.
Mr President, Commissioner, allow me to begin by congratulating the rapporteur, Mr Schwab, and Mrs Gerhard, on their excellent work.
And a general reflection.
It is not a question of increasing protectionism, but rather of not cutting rights that already exist in some of the Member States.
Because if we want to establish a genuine internal market for relations between businesses and consumers, we must
to establish, at the same time, a balance between the high level of protection of consumer rights and the defence of the competitiveness of companies.
This balance involves agreeing a minimum level of harmonisation and allowing Member States the possibility of maintaining or adopting additional regulations that improve consumer protection.
We cannot allow the adoption of a Community standard to mean that many consumers and users are left unprotected.
I welcome the fact that the negotiations in the European Parliament have improved, but I would like to stress that the rights already established so far should not be cut.

Mr President, many experts are telling us that the next crisis could be an energy crisis and that, either we are not doing the necessary things to avoid it or we are not doing them quickly enough to avoid it.
Next Saturday, the World Forum for Nature is calling on us all to make a small gesture in the name of sustainability.
Many citizens will turn off the lights for one hour and many public administrations will follow suit.
But that is not enough.
Europe cannot continue to depend on energy sources that do not belong to it, that are far away or that are insecure.
We have to help Member States to build a new energy model in which renewable energies are at the heart of the new model.
We must also promote awareness of new consumption patterns, because renewable energy and efficiency are undoubtedly the keys to future sustainability.

Finally, I would like to call on all the countries of the Union to join that call, that will of five Member States, including Spain, to increase the reduction in CO emissions to 30%, because that way we would show that Europe, in addition to being at the forefront in terms of freedom and rights, is also at the forefront in its commitment to the sustainability of the planet.
Madam President, most of the times I have spoken in this House, I have done so to denounce violence against women, and once again I am here to combat it.

I must say that not only am I not tired of it, but I consider it essential to continue speaking publicly about this human rights violation, since no cause can be defended without a sounding board, and this Parliament is the best

megaphone at my disposal to defend all those women who suffer abuse on a daily basis and who need us legislators to force the Member States to punish the perpetrators in accordance with the seriousness of the crime and also to help the victims.

When we consider that one in four European women have suffered gender-based violence at least once in their lives, and that children have suffered it to the same extent, I think it is impossible not to demand a greater commitment, such as that recognised by the Commission in its action plan.

I think that we need to keep insisting, again and again, on putting victims at the heart of things, as we already did in the European protection order for victims, on which I was rapporteur, and which I hope will become a reality as soon as possible.
This report has enjoyed broad consensus and support from the outset, which shows that when it comes to tackling domestic violence, which affects all members of society,
families, children, older people, women and men, should not be a matter for political opportunism, as we should all be honoured to make progress in the solution of this social scourge.

The first thing is not to keep quiet, and the second thing is to get to work, because although we have been talking about gender violence for decades, we have not been able to reduce the number of deaths.

We must be doing something wrong, and it is therefore time to forget about demagogy and to think and to move from words to deeds, providing more real and necessary aid so that women can live with dignity and security.
