security. I want all victims to know that I will always be here to continue giving them my voice until we all succeed in defeating this evil.
terrible enemy that so sadly takes the lives of so many innocent people. I say: enough is enough, and I ask all of you to say it with me.
Mr President, Commissioner, I agree with you that we need to listen to citizens and regain their confidence in the European project.
Therefore, today could be a great day for Europe and for European social integration.

With the adoption of these reports, we will not only move forward towards reactivating the single market, a key element in tackling the current economic crisis, but we will also respond to citizens' demands for more Europe and for stronger social rights.
Europe must respond to the new global challenges and make the European economy a highly competitive social market economy that creates significant growth with more and better jobs.

However, this objective must be based on the inclusion of a social clause in all legislation relating to the internal market, which places citizens at the heart of our attention and priorities, and strengthens their social rights.
Finally, I would like to ask the Commission to present these proposals as soon as possible.
Mr President, bluetongue has been a constant threat to our livestock and, with it, to our public health.

a disease against which we were fighting through the use of the active vaccine, which requires the creation of restricted zones for animal movement, because the use of these vaccines can cause unwanted circulation of the virus in non-infected animals.

For these reasons, the use of the vaccine was limited to those areas where infected animals had previously been found, thus creating a double disadvantage for farmers: on the one hand, the disease and, on the other, the restrictions.

All these problems seem to be avoidable thanks to the new inactivated vaccine, which, in addition to avoiding the disturbances that could be caused in the livestock internal market, allows new and broader prevention strategies to be developed, which is what is really important in the fight against this disease.
For all these reasons, it is vital that we amend the directive to allow its use, and that we do so as soon as possible.

Ladies and gentlemen, compliance with our Treaties, especially with regard to the equivalence tables, cannot be used as an excuse for this legislation not to enter into force before the summer, because with our delay, with your delay, we would only be harming livestock farmers.

Mr President, first of all, I would like to thank the Committee on Petitions for the work it has done following the complaint submitted by Fepex, the Spanish Association of Fruit and Vegetable Producers and Exporters, based on the report published in 2007 by the European Anti-Fraud Office (OLAF).

I think the conclusions of that report are very clear and more than justify the fact that the European Commission should have got to work a long time ago to clarify how the entry price system works, at least as far as the tomato sector is concerned.

However, the Commission has not only failed to intervene to solve the possible frauds that could arise from the current system of entry prices, but has also negotiated a new agricultural agreement with Morocco without having previously resolved the shortcomings of the current import regime.

Ladies and gentlemen, it is high time that the European Commission took its responsibilities seriously and began to investigate where the fraud is taking place and, if necessary, demand the recovery of unpaid customs duties.
I think that we should in no way ratify a new agreement with Morocco until the details we have mentioned have been resolved.

Our objective is not to slow down the signing of international agreements, but rather to ensure that these agreements do not seriously harm the interests of European production and that the rules signed in these agreements are respected.

With regard to the new agreement with Morocco, the European fruit and vegetables sector is only asking for guarantees that the agreed measures will be effectively implemented, and this is only possible through reform of the current import pricing system.
We cannot have a new agreement with Morocco until we have a new entry price system.
We will not be able to ratify that agreement that the Commission has already concluded.

Moreover, if it is the Member States' responsibility to carry out this control, they should be required to make a significant economic effort. However, it is also true that we have been calling for a long time for a European border and customs system, on the same lines as that of the United States, to ensure effective control.

Mr President, Commissioner, this report has been complex to deal with in Parliament, mainly because it involves measures that require the approval of the World Trade Organisation, and the preliminary proposal from the Commission that was sent to Parliament is different from the one sent to Geneva.
We believe that it is necessary to help Pakistan. We understand that, exceptionally, some trade preferences can be granted, provided that the sensitivities of the
European industry and, in particular, the textile sector, which in communities such as the Valencian Community in Spain is suffering the consequences of the crisis very seriously.

However, this report has shown us that the measures that can be taken within the framework of international trade probably cannot be dealt with as urgently as these acts require, so we must learn lessons for the future.

We should help Pakistan, yes, but not with the Commission's original proposals, since they do not take into account the effects on European industry, in terms of the time limits for the application of sensitive products, and
would even delay the application of these measures, as they would not coincide with the pending text scheduled to be approved by the WTO, and we would be out of time.
We therefore consider it unacceptable to maintain this proposal, and we think we have to vote against it.
Mr President, I would like to begin by congratulating everyone who has made it possible to reach an agreement.
This is an agreement that we should all welcome, because it affects the daily lives of all citizens, but in the constructive spirit that characterises me, I would like to make a few points.
Firstly, I would like to draw attention to the importance of providing accurate, relevant information, but above all, comprehensible and uniform information
for consumers on the components of textile products. All the information in the world will be of no use if citizens are unable to understand it.
We have taken a positive step, which is necessary but not sufficient. We should go further and protect citizens who could become more vulnerable.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is essential to establish a European system for labelling sizes based on body measurements, because the first thing we come across when we go to buy a garment is the size.
Therefore, we should be much more ambitious and seek harmonisation of sizes.
Mr President, I have three comments on the situation surrounding cucumbers.
I am German by descent and I have great affection for that country, but it is clear that the German authorities have acted precipitately and imprudently in this case.

On the other hand, if we analyse the European response, we see that the one carried out by the European Food Safety Authority and the Commission has been feeble, very muted and vague; it has not known how to coordinate or lead.
Thirdly, the victims have the right to economic compensation quickly.
For the time being, I propose to Parliament that we organise a major European gastronomy exhibition in Brussels or here in Strasbourg, based precisely on the cucumber.
We must reclaim lost honour, to use the words of the German writer Heinrich Böll. We must reclaim the lost honour of cucumber producers.
Europe is the right place for this.
Mr President, first of all, I would like to express my sincere condolences for the 23 victims already suffered, 22 in Germany and one in Sweden.
This crisis is the most obvious proof that the European Union must improve its food alert system and its internal coordination, and improve it greatly.
The Member States involved have demonstrated slowness, ineffectiveness and inaction, and at this point, even after 23 deaths, we still do not know where the problem is coming from.

The European Commission must seriously reflect on the problems caused by its irresponsible actions or inaction in times of crisis such as the current one, causing cases of economic ruin and destruction of jobs that were previously

unstable, unemployment, helplessness and, of course, a health crisis with extremely high health risks for the population. Because while you in the Commission were echoing the irresponsible statements made by regional authorities

very misinformed and rather irresponsible German media, named Spanish farms by name. Meanwhile, the population remained exposed to the bacterium without knowing it. Believing they were avoiding it, citizens were more exposed than ever to eating something that could harm them.

The European Commission should today, at the Agriculture Council taking place in Luxembourg at 2 p.m., propose to ministers that part of the EUR 2 500 million in unutilised surplus funds from the common agricultural policy be used.
those EUR 2.5 billion to repair as much as possible the damage caused.

To do this, we need to find a legal basis that allows us to do so, a legal basis that has already been found on other occasions, such as the mad cow disease and dioxins cases, and that should be found now.
Therefore, Commissioners, Ministers, you have to live up to your responsibilities here in Luxembourg.

The German authorities, in view of the irresponsible accusations made, should also support this economic solution without a doubt and from the outset, and also finance with their own resources communication campaigns to re-establish consumer confidence, which has been lost in stone fruit production.

Mr President, Commissioner, nothing that we do here or in the European Union will bring the dead back to life, but hopefully what is being done will prevent more deaths and will stop the chain of patients who are suffering without having done anything to deserve it.

We do not know the causes for certain, but we do know some things that we must call by their name: there has been political negligence and there has been negligence on the part of the German Federal Government. It cannot act as if Hamburg were a city-state on another continent.

The arrogance that Mrs Merkel and her Minister of Health have shown in addressing other countries with such unusual frequency, if it had been translated into diligence and efficiency in coordinating, some of the things that have happened would not have happened.

This negligence has had criminal consequences for a State, which has been Spain, and for its most important sector, agriculture, and has led to the ruin of thousands of people and small entrepreneurs and has discredited the economy for a long time.
I ask you to go to the Council and ask for full compensation for those affected, as we cannot ask for lives for those who have died.
and that control systems be reviewed.
And that Mrs Merkel should be held accountable.
We should ask them to show less arrogance and more diligence.
He must be held responsible for the damage he has caused.
Hamburg cannot ignore that it is in its state.
They cannot ignore their responsibilities.
It cannot hide behind a decentralised and federal State to cause the damage it has caused.

A little more humility, a little more good management and a little less conservatism would have been very useful so that this crisis did not get out of hand and so that Spain and other southern farmers did not have to suffer its painful consequences.
Review the controls, review the alert systems and demand accountability, because we in Spain are asking for it, Commissioner, from you, from the Council and from the German State.
Mr President, we are facing a public health problem, an epidemic, as the Commissioner said, and in this case, we are looking for culprits, not causes.

The system has not worked: we have 22 dead, 1 600 affected, and we still do not know the cause three weeks later. And, appealing to the precautionary principle without scientific basis – I repeat, without scientific basis – Spain is being blamed.
Ladies and gentlemen, Spanish cucumbers were not contaminated.
contaminated, they were not contaminated. I repeat this because I have heard some contradictory versions here.
But in order for you to see the importance of what these words have triggered, 300 000 jobs could be affected, and there will be a loss of confidence.
An epidemic knows no borders.

I agree with you, Commissioner: it knows no borders. We are asking for responsibility for what has not been done, for what we must do immediately, because Germany has a risk problem at the moment: it has the highest number of affected people, but Spain is not to blame.
We are asking for compensation.
The most important thing, and what brings us together here today, is the victims, because no one should feel as unprotected as we consumers feel today.
It is out of respect for the victims that we need clarity and responsibility.

It is my responsibility to denounce the German authorities who unjustly and hastily accused Spanish cucumbers and, specifically, the cooperatives in Málaga and Almería, which were model farms and which will not recover from these accusations.
Thousands of families without jobs are asking why, why? They need an answer, and above all, they want to know what their future will be, not only in Andalusia but throughout Europe's farming regions.

Therefore, and trusting that the experts will find a quick solution, I call on the Commission to ask for immediate and moral economic measures for all the producers who, from Andalusia and from all over Europe, look to us with indignation but also with hope.
Instead of prioritising European research and cooperation, the German authorities have slandered and harmed cucumber producers and others.
And still no one in Germany has resigned or offered reparations commensurate with the damage caused.
It is Germany that has a serious credibility problem.
Mrs Merkel's treatment of the countries of the South in agricultural and financial matters is intolerable.
Someone said that Merkel was a disciple of Adenauer and Kohl.
None of that is credible.
Mr President, it is no coincidence that Spain, Italy, Greece, Ireland, Portugal and every other country share our reluctance to accept the so-called Eurovignette on a voluntary basis.

The coincidence, the criticism, is due to our condition as peripheral countries, which means that, in the end, we will be the ones paying this unusual tax, which will be collected by the European Union countries that have geographical centrality.
The rationale behind the Eurovignette involves great contradictions and the supporting argument is becoming increasingly flimsy.

Although we accept the rationality of internalising external costs as a starting point, in the end we do not support penalising heavy goods vehicles alone to alleviate congestion, leaving passenger cars out, in the same way that the old slogan of 'the polluter pays' is applied in a discriminatory manner.

On the other hand, ladies and gentlemen, the eurovignette, which was envisaged as a source of funding for infrastructure, will ultimately only have 15% allocated to funding trans-European networks, and not in a mandatory way.

but as a mere recommendation. In this way, the Minister of Finance on duty will allocate the funds collected in the way he considers most appropriate to the interests of his country, within the broad area of infrastructure.
The end result will be nothing other than another stick to beat the road haulage industry with, when today it is the only mode that is making the internal market work.
This is the reality, ladies and gentlemen, a reality that is much harsher in a time of economic crisis.

In this respect, the competitiveness of European products in world markets cannot afford experiments that could further worsen the precarious situation of the European economy by imposing new additional costs.

The food crisis, which has temerariously been made to fall on Spanish products, has produced an unfair penalty and huge losses for the very people that the Eurovignette penalises: hauliers and farmers of primary crops.
These are the reasons, ladies and gentlemen, why the Spanish delegation of the Group of the European People ’ s Party (Christian Democrats) will vote against this draft directive.
You will all understand, dear colleagues, that when I take the chair of this sitting, I express my sadness and dismay at the death of Jorge Semprún, which occurred a few hours ago at the age of 87.

On Jorge Semprún, we celebrate the man who was imprisoned in the Buchenwald concentration and extermination camp, the fighter against the forces of Nazism in France and against the Franco dictatorship in my country, Spain.

We also celebrate the very distinguished writer who is committed, and who has collaborated in particular in our European Book of the Year programme, and we also celebrate the committed European politician.

I mourn his death as a colleague and friend, and I am going to ask President Buzek to send a message of condolence and solidarity to his family and to the Spanish Government, which I am sure we all share here.
the creation of an economic governance area governed by federalism principles adapted to European integration.
We cannot go on turning a blind eye.
It is incoherent to have a single currency and at the same time lack the fiscal and financial instruments that this currency requires.
Specifically, therefore, we need the issue of European debt, a resounding no to competition between the debt issues of the Member States, the creation of a genuine European public treasury, the
harmonisation of tax policies that guarantee equality among citizens, combat tax fraud and help to define a progressive European social policy based on effective public services.
These ideas are – I am sure you will agree – basic.
But we have to repeat them again and again, because nobody listens to them.
And precisely because they do not listen, the dark times that the poet Bertolt Brecht spoke of are approaching.

Mr President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, the current economic and financial crisis we are experiencing highlights the need for regional policy as the best instrument for reducing the development differences between regions and Member States.
At the same time, we can restore the positive image of Europe, which lately citizens only associate with tough adjustment plans and social cuts.

The future cohesion policy, the pillar of solidarity of the European project and social justice, should take into account the following priorities: first, reinforcing social cohesion and integrating the challenges posed by the Europe 2020 strategy; second, taking into account the needs and characteristics

geographical features of the regions, especially the most disadvantaged ones. Thirdly, we must maintain the principle of the gradual reduction in the receipt of funds so that regions do not abandon the convergence objective, avoiding abrupt changes in the receipt of aid. Fourthly, we must promote a system
equitable.
Finally, ladies and gentlemen, the authorities are applying tough budgetary adjustment policies.
The support of the Union is therefore necessary.
I hope the Council takes good note.

in defence of fundamental rights, I call on the responsible institutions to review the designation of San Sebastián as European Capital of Culture, because I believe that it was chosen on the basis of a political criterion, which constitutes discrimination against the other candidate cities.

The President of the Court said that she had been chosen to support the peace process, and as far as I know, in Spain there is a terrorist organisation called ETA which has not laid down its arms to date and which, two months ago, managed to get its political wing, Bildu, to govern, among other municipalities, San Sebastián.
I do not think that Europe should give such a powerful megaphone to those who do not condemn terrorist violence and will spread propaganda to apportion blame between victims and murderers.
Where is the right to freedom and dignity?
Certainly not in San Sebastián, where our colleagues, such as Mayor Oreja, Iturgaiz, Salva or myself, must go escorted.
I believe in the culture of life, not in the culture of the shot.
Can there be true culture without freedom?
No, and a thousand times no.
When San Sebastián is a free city for all, let it submit its candidacy again, and in the meantime, let them learn the culture of respect for those who think differently.
Because tomorrow they may deserve it, but today, with Batasuna and its heirs in the institutions, they do not deserve it.
Mr President, first of all, I would like to thank the shadow rapporteurs for their fruitful cooperation in drafting it.
The same applies to the other Members of this Parliament who are members of the committee.
Sixty years ago, the European Coal and Steel Community was created. Nothing better to commemorate this event than to debate energy infrastructure in Europe today.

Today, the European Union has clearly defined objectives for its policy: a common European internal market, promoting renewable energies as a means or instrument for combating climate change, security of supply and diversification of its sources.
Title XXI of the Treaty of Lisbon has been dedicated to energy, where these commitments that I have just mentioned are formulated, as well as the promotion of interconnection of energy networks.
The objective of the report we have drawn up is to correct energy infrastructures that, in the Commission's opinion, are outdated and poorly interconnected.
For example, we see a spectacular increase in electricity generation from wind power in the North Sea and the Baltic Sea, with the enormous potential for renewable energy in southern
Europe and in North Africa, with the problems arising from the large-scale storage of electricity, with the need to recharge electric vehicles, among many other urgent issues.
All of this is impossible to tackle today unless we devise new networks and new connections, both within the European Union and with third countries.
In the years to come, therefore, we need to move forward in determining the necessary infrastructure by establishing the criteria that will allow us to select projects of European interest.
b) eliminating energy islands, c) ensuring that these projects are implemented within reasonable timeframes and finding solutions to the contentious issue of the
authorisation and licensing procedures in the Member States; and (d) ensuring financing that attracts and promotes private investment, without prejudice to the public support that may be necessary.
Energy efficiency and saving, as well as smart grids, are central ingredients in the new design incorporated into the report, because we have
believe that energy demand reduction can reduce dependency on imports and the consequent impact on infrastructure needs.
The main concern of this report from the very beginning has been the processing of the permits and authorisations needed to allow the necessary work to be carried out.
are now becoming eternal.
and they are prolonged by the opposition of the populations to seeing towers or other types of installations grow in their vicinity.

To resolve this conflict, early information for citizens has been considered, as well as establishing an alert system in the event that the authorities of a Member State do not respond to a request within a reasonable time.
We are facing ambitious objectives for which everyone ’ s cooperation is needed.
In this respect, the companies in the sector, which are rightly calling for a stable regulatory framework, are essential, as this is the best way to ensure the financing of their efforts.
So far, the European Union 's energy policy, both towards the outside world and internally, has lacked a truly European impetus.
Europe must speak with one voice, a voice that is clearly audible and resounds throughout the edifice of Europe, because it is from our energy fragility that we must

mobilise our forces to build an energetic Europe that is solid and vigorous, worthy successor, in this anniversary year, to that great invention that was the European Coal and Steel Community, our very cradle.

Mr President, every time we reach an agreement that allows us to move forward, we should congratulate ourselves, but I do not think that the agreement we have reached is one that is worth dying of happiness.
We must be prudent in the moments of happiness we have.

It is true that we have made progress, especially in relation to food labelling, especially in relation to safety and clarity of the message, but we have fallen short on trans fats and, for example, on the obligation to label them.
What I do want to ask the Commission is not to run out the clock.
