We must improve our safety nets and make them more flexible and effective.
We must work to further integrate the sector into the value chain, ensure that quotas are not distributed, particularly in certain areas, and promote the quality policy.

But without a doubt, with our positive vote and with everything that remains to be done, today many farmers will be able to see in this Parliament that their right to work, the most basic of all, has been defended, which is the right to be able to live with dignity from the income generated by their work.
Mr B has rightly highlighted the disastrous consequences that the approval of this agreement would entail.
This agreement, which increases the quotas for certain horticultural products, makes no sense at all when the figures of the current agreement are not respected.

It is worth remembering that the Spanish Federation of Fruit and Vegetable Exporters complained to the Committee on Petitions of this very Parliament about the massive entry of tomatoes through Perpignan at a price below that set in the agreement.
Illegal situations such as the one described place European farmers at a competitive disadvantage.
I must also add that our farmers must comply with strict quality, environmental and social standards that our Moroccan partners do not always respect.
Finally, I will mention the question of the Sahara.
According to the United Nations Charter, the Sahara is an autonomous territory and the exploitation of its resources must benefit the local population.
Furthermore, it is an area over which the United Nations does not recognise the sovereignty of the Kingdom of Morocco.
The European Union must ensure that international law is complied with.

I am in favour of an agreement with Morocco, an agreement that establishes rigorous control mechanisms from the European Union in relation to quotas and prices, an agreement that fights against dumping, that does not exploit Moroccan farmers and, above all, that gives dignity to our European farmers.
This agreement is certainly not that.

Last year, the Petitions Committee of the European Parliament requested that the European Commission amend the import price regime for fruit and vegetables in the Union, a regime that is facilitating the fraudulent entry of products from third countries, as the Union's anti-fraud agency, the OLAF, itself states.
They are mainly Moroccan tomatoes.
The same request was included in a declaration signed last year by the Governments of Spain, France and Italy.
This fraud is resolved by amending the implementing regulation of the common organisation of the agricultural market, because aligning the banana code does not solve the problem, Commissioner.

For several years, I have been defending within the Group of the European People ’ s Party (Christian Democrats) the need to freeze new concessions to Morocco until the European Union equips itself with the necessary instruments to guarantee compliance with the current agreement.
The European Commission argues that it is the Member States that must guarantee control of imports.

However, what the Commission does not say is that the system of entry prices that is in force prevents national customs authorities from properly tracing the prices declared by importers to check that there are no irregularities.

If they amend the implementing regulation of the OCM for agricultural products, we will have solved part of the problem, because otherwise the legal loophole in European legislation prevents national authorities from doing their job properly.
Once again, dear colleagues, the debate on a summit.
Many have been held, all announced with great fanfare, trying to create an expectation that is smaller every day.
They are losing credibility because they are not helping to solve the crisis.
Summits where the same agreements are reiterated time and again.

It should be known that, unless political union advances and this Parliament and the executive power of the European Commission are strengthened, summits will be repeated but the economic crisis will worsen.
Two ideas, briefly. Firstly, we need to stress that responsible solidarity and fulfilling our duties are the basis
materials with which Europe is built. The second is that the use of unexpended Structural Funds should be immediately authorised.
These amounts could easily combat the figures for youth unemployment in Europe.

Madam President, today, from the European Parliament, I want to pay tribute to all women. Although we have come a long way since March 1911, when women took to the streets to demand the vote and better working conditions, I believe that gender equality is still a long way off.
Today, women can work without having to ask anyone for permission, but in many cases they still earn less than men for doing similar work, which is unacceptable.
We must also remember rural women, whose work is ignored.
I believe that another uncomfortable reality, but one that we must continue to denounce, is violence against women.

This year alone, an excessive number of women have been murdered, not to mention the serious threats from those who believe they have the right to commit physical or psychological violence against them.
It is time to say 'enough is enough' and to say it loud and clear.
From this Parliament, I reiterate my commitment to the European Protection Order for victims and to the Victims Directive presented by the Commission, which, God willing, will be voted on next June.
I would also like to point out that motherhood continues to be an obstacle to women ' s career progression, and I believe that this is a trend that we need to change.
I want to send out a message of hope: with many women and with the help of an increasing number of men, we must move forward along the path of equality of opportunity, rights and also duties.
We have come a long way, but there is still a lot to do.
I have to admit that I do not believe in quotas at all, but I have to say that something has to be done, and I agree with
many of my fellow Members and, of course, Mrs Reding, for opening this debate and moving forward on this path so that women can regain the place they deserve. You can count on my support.

Mr President, in a time of economic crisis and budget cuts that is affecting public health, and in which, as in the case of the region where I live, research centres such as the Prince Felipe Centre, which specialises in diabetes, are left without funds, this resolution could not come at a better time. It is absolutely appropriate.

We need a common European strategy against diabetes and we need a Council recommendation telling the Member States that they have to increase research, including with stem cells, that they have to coordinate their results in order to optimise them, and that they have to establish programmes for healthy habits, diet and lifestyles.

In short, we must put people's health first and, in this case, investigate, coordinate and develop national programmes coordinated by the Commission and demanding results on diabetes, in order to guarantee the quality of life of Europeans and to guarantee their lives.

If we spend more money on this, some people will get paid a little later, but those who are having problems will have a better quality of life, and we, as the Council and the Commission, will be fulfilling one of our obligations, which is to look after the health of our citizens.
Mr President, of the many issues raised by this debate, I would like to highlight the emissions trading market, a market which certainly started with defects.
However, today, the limitation of rights and the economic crisis have meant that the price of carbon has been drastically reduced, in other words, it is so low that it is cheap to pollute.
It is therefore urgent to reform the design of a policy on that market, because its deterioration threatens all green efforts, all the European Union ’ s green policy.
If emissions allowances are not valued, we are encouraging an increase in emissions. Polluting industries will not invest in new techniques and, what is worse,
The funding of important projects, such as those relating to energy efficiency or carbon storage plants, will be put at risk.
This is the risk.
It is up to us, to the Commission and to Parliament, to prevent it.
Mr President, few asylum seekers are permanently resettled in Europe.
My country, Spain, has barely begun the experience.
The cases are, however, of urgent necessity.
The Members have confirmed this on the ground during visits to the Iraqi refugee camps of Palestinian origin in Syria. We also received news from Tunisia straight away.
Another institutional aspect is the balance of power between Parliament and the Council.
Our rapporteur, the brave Ruiz-Tavares. There are many good MEPs, but few with such tenacity as Ruiz-Tavares.
Tavares. He has fought for the European Parliament to maintain the new powers it gained following the adoption of the Treaty of Lisbon.
I cannot forget the constant work carried out by AGLUR, without which success would not have been possible either.
Ag Nur has made us all aware of the urgency of the report so that the funds can be used as soon as possible, now that they are so needed.
Finally, Commissioner, thank you very much and I hope that we can also give you and your good heart the satisfaction of getting the asylum package through next month.

The European social model of which we are so proud is not based solely on our pension, education or healthcare systems, but also on a way of understanding the balance between employer and employee within companies.

The labour reform that the Spanish Government has approved without dialogue and without consensus breaks with that social model and moves us away from the objectives of the 2020 strategy, both in terms of job creation and in terms of the quality of employment.
Labour reform on its own does not create jobs: economic growth does.
What labour law does do is define the conditions under which workers enter companies, the conditions under which they remain in them and the conditions under which they leave them.
And with this reform, where 80% of workers will be unprotected because they will not be covered by collective agreements, where they can be dismissed because of a fall in company income or
can unilaterally modify their schedules. What is clear is that workers will enter with greater insecurity, remain in the labour market with more fear and leave with less compensation.

That is why this reform, which undermines the European social model and promotes the competitiveness of the Spanish economy solely in relation to workers' conditions, deserves a general strike like the one that is being called for tomorrow in Spain in defence of this social model.

Mr President, Mr Barroso, let me say at the outset that the Spanish delegation of the Group of the European People's Party (Christian Democrats) considers that the two packages you have presented today, both the employment package and the aid to Greece, are correct and are in line with the consistency of your approach.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is undeniable that we are living through difficult times and the events of each day remind us that the crisis is far from over.
Spain has inevitably been put in the spotlight.
The markets have rightly raised the alarm, encouraged in part by statements which, in my opinion, Mr President, were untimely.
Some are in the context of important elections, in which both candidates cite my country as a dialectical element of confrontation.
Of course, we do not appreciate any of the references.
On the other hand, some people, in order to divert attention from their own problems, also deal with us in a politically undelicate manner.
Some people have not been very tactful here today, as the Italians would say, and there has been a certain lack of political delicacy.
Ladies and gentlemen, the situation in the markets does not reflect the strength of Spain.
We are a solvent country with the full capacity to meet our debts.
Spain ’ s debt remains lower than the EU average, and the Spanish Government is fully committed to meeting the deficit targets agreed with the European Union.
Mr Boguda, Mr Rajoy has never said that he cannot meet the objectives set by the European Union.
It has said the exact opposite: that it is prepared to meet each and every one of those objectives, and it reiterates this every day.

The Spanish Government has adopted, and will continue to adopt, very ambitious measures to guarantee fiscal consolidation and lay the foundations for improved competitiveness, job creation and growth, in the few months that it has been in office.
The IMF forecasts, by the way, of yesterday, the IMF itself admits, are prior to the analysis of the decisions and measures taken by the Spanish Government at present.

Other measures that we have adopted: the State general budget for 2012, which, in our opinion, will guarantee that the deficit target is met this year; the Organic Budgetary Stability Act; this month,

a reform of education and health, which will involve an adjustment of EUR 10 billion; the reform of the financial sector will also undoubtedly mean that financial institutions have to provide more than EUR 50 billion
In short, Spain is meeting the objectives set by the Union and by the Commission, and what we are asking for is responsibility from everyone.

We all need to save ourselves, and Spain, with everyone else, without a doubt, and the criticism from some or others contributes nothing. Either we are all countries capable of working together and contributing to growth and new employment, or else it will be difficult for us to move forward in the terms proposed by the Commission.
Mr Weber, do you want to make Mr Rajoy, the President of the Spanish Government, the star of this show?
I am in favour of it. I would have no objection.
But Mr Rajoy is not the protagonist of the crisis.

The main cause of the crisis is the legacy left by the Spanish socialist, communist and green left, which has left the worst legacy that any government has ever left, when they received the best legacy that any government has ever had.
Mr President, this morning I read in an important German newspaper that Spain and Argentina have a conflict relating to an oil company.
This is precisely the wrong way to look at the matter.
The conflict is between Europe and Argentina, and beyond that, with that great European value which is legal certainty and its respect in international trade relations.
The European institutions have excellent jurists who can find the appropriate instruments in primary or derived Community law to neutralise the arbitrariness of the Argentine authorities.
I am a modest professor of public law and I offer to collaborate with them.
The credibility of the European Union in the eyes of its citizens is at stake.

Mr President, the Committee on Agriculture and Rural Development shares the ultimate objective of this directive, which is to try to use taxation to influence the composition of our energy mix, but we are facing a very complex debate.

full of thorns in economic, social and environmental terms. What is important is to maintain those balances that will allow us to make more progress in environmental terms, yes, but that will not harm us economically and will not create greater social crises than the ones we are already experiencing.
This is the case with agriculture.

As everyone knows, energy costs are increasingly representing a very important part of the fixed costs of our farms, at a time when we are also discussing the crisis in the raw materials markets, the prospect of a loss of subsidies and increasing health and environmental requirements, all of which are reducing their profitability.
That is why we have basically asked for two things.

The first is the maintenance of zero-rated deductions in the energy component, now linked to the drafting and implementation of energy efficiency and savings strategies in rural areas, and the inclusion of the
agriculture within the sectors considered to be at risk of carbon leakage, so that they can also benefit from the allowances permitted in the CO emissions phase.
In other words, a more environmentally demanding policy, but one that maintains the balances that make our rural environment viable at a time of change and uncertainty.
Mr President, we are living through a major controversy, which has as its essential reference points the concepts of austerity and growth.
If you listen to the noise being made, you would think that the idea of growth is a brilliant new invention.
That is further from the truth than anything else.
In Europe, the Stability and Growth Pact has been in force since 1997.

That is why a large part of the policies carried out by the European institutions, such as regional cohesion and research policies, are growth-oriented policies that aim to promote development and citizens' welfare.

Therefore, and I am addressing the Commission and those Members of this Parliament who have the greatest opportunity to be heard, we should not feel in the least bit shy when we take part in that debate, which we can face with our heads held high.
This is my minority opinion among the non-attached members.
Madam President, first of all, let me thank all my colleagues and, in particular, the shadow rapporteurs for their help with this report, which has made it much stronger.

We all know that the twentieth anniversary of the launch of the single market is being celebrated, which must be the driving force behind economic activity and growth in Europe, and key to getting out of the crisis and achieving the objectives of Europe 2020.
In these twenty years, the development of the internal market must be compatible with its development, with a broad framework for consumer protection, growth and rights that generate dynamism.
and trust. Transparency, a basic principle of democracy and good governance, which Commissioner Barnier referred to this afternoon, and cooperation between the Commission and Parliament.
It is now time to develop the regulatory framework relating to the protection and training of consumers, and particularly of vulnerable consumers.
We know that the diversity of situations of vulnerability, both endogenous and exogenous, makes it difficult to adopt a global legislative instrument.
This requires us to tackle the problem of vulnerability from a cross-cutting analysis that takes into account the different needs, capabilities and circumstances of consumers.
Aware of the current shortcomings, the Commission has focused its strategy on improving consumer information and education.
Although it is an ambitious proposal, it is not enough to protect vulnerable consumers, because their vulnerability stems precisely from their lack of assertiveness and understanding of the

information they receive or the options available to them or due to their lack of knowledge of the existing complaint and compensation systems, especially in the case of cross-border and online purchases.

That is why, in order to solve the shortcomings, that is to say the problems, it is necessary to adopt a strategy to strengthen vulnerable consumers, and for both the Commission and the Member States to introduce specific measures into their legislation to guarantee adequate protection

go beyond mere information, particularly in those areas where the level has been shown to be higher and where the greatest number of consumer complaints are registered: the financial sector, whose complexity means that any consumer could potentially be vulnerable.

The financial services industry is therefore being asked to redouble its efforts to provide comprehensible, truthful information, giving clear explanations and using straightforward language to describe the nature of the products and services it offers. In the case of the food sector, in relation to advertising

of foods high in fat, salt and sugar, and to children and young people, who are the most sensitive to advertising for these foods; the transport sector, which still often has difficulties when vulnerable people travel; the energy and telecommunications sector;

where lack of transparency causes additional difficulties for consumers in some cases, for example, in the energy sector, when it comes to determining the tariff that best suits their needs, or in access to justice, where consumers may find it difficult to access the mechanisms
existing.
Therefore, we call on the Commission and on the Member States – and allow me to draw the attention of the Spanish Minister of Justice, Mr Gallardón – not to roll back rights.
and go against the current trend in these difficult times: to use intelligence and good governance to promote an alternative resolution of disputes in the area of consumption.

Finally, I would like to conclude by saying that any one of us could become a vulnerable consumer. Thank you very much, and I would like to thank all the shadow rapporteurs for their help in strengthening this report.
Mr President, Commissioner, I must say that I have a feeling of near desperation, of frustration, which I must share with someone.
I hope you will allow me to do so this afternoon.

This is the third or fourth debate we have had in this House recently on youth unemployment across Europe, and debate after debate, while youth unemployment rates are soaring in some Member States, we are already close to

50% of the rate of the working population, while many of them are already left without any kind of social or unemployment coverage, while all this is happening, while we go from a situation that is evolving from worrying to alarming.

untenable and from the untenable to the dramatic. The Commission once again presents itself before this Parliament, before this Chamber, as if it were a political commentator, as if it were just another analyst, and not as the true government of the Union, which is what it is and which is what we need.
Commissioner, to govern is to set priorities, to take the initiative with the aim of achieving some end, some objective, and the Commission is not doing that.

Governance is not about carrying out a simple restructuring of funds, or even just announcing it to wash your hands in front of public opinion, but about developing an ambitious, lasting and rapid strategy to end youth unemployment.

To govern is not to resign yourself to a youth unemployment rate of over 20 % and to be satisfied with a pilot project of EUR 4 million, which is ridiculous in the light of the situation and the magnitude of the problem we are facing.

To govern is not to give in to the pressure from the College of Commissioners or from other Member States of the Council, which are trying to convince us that the only way out of this financial crisis, of this new wave of recession, is to give up on wages, labour rights or compensation, and that we as a generation can only have a future of insecurity.

Because if this were true, Commissioner, that we only gain competitiveness through precariousness and that we only become more competitive by being more precarious, Zimbabwe would be the most competitive country, and it is not.
Sweden, Finland and Denmark are, precisely, characterised by a model of labour relations and also by a model of labour rights and guarantees.
Mr President, employment is much more than a civil right: it is a means of connecting with the system, a means of social integration.

Therefore, the more unemployment there is, the greater the political disenchantment. That is why, Commissioner, I do not accept your arguments about competition issues as an excuse for inaction on youth unemployment.

Moreover, Commissioner, when you have had to interpret the Treaties loosely in order to create, for example, the Financial Stability Fund, you have done so, and when we have had to amend the Treaties in order to comply with or satisfy the deliriums of some Member State on the issue of austerity, we have done so as well.
So what am I trying to tell you?
There, for example, is the Youth Guarantee, which I do not need to explain to you.

Fight to increase the budget. Use the European Social Fund as an instrument if necessary. Design a mechanism for implementation that is co-financed and solidarity-based, and that helps most those who have the most unemployment. Press for this social assistance policy to be changed even more in the countries worst affected. And remember that behind every figure there is a person who is having a hard time.
Then you will think like me that what matters least is who has the competence, because for the citizens, especially
Madam President, Commissioner, I would like to thank the Committee on International Trade for its work, and the rapporteurs and shadow rapporteurs.

In general terms, we are dealing with a good GSP + regulation, which is necessary, particularly in certain actions to control the import rules for textile products that could harm production in the European Union.
However, we are concerned about the exclusion from the GSP in 2014 of certain countries classified as high middle income, countries such as Latin America.
What happens with these middle-income countries that have undiversified exports?
diversified. Why not introduce a diversification criterion for exports?
This could result in two speeds, with countries such as Costa Rica, Cuba and Ecuador benefiting from it, while potentially developing countries such as China and Brazil lag behind.

Therefore, I believe that we should support a transition period and that amendments will be tabled with this transition period, to adapt to this situation, to these countries, and to give the possibility of opening negotiations and trade agreements with them, so that they can benefit from them.

Mr President, I supported Mrs Nedelcheva ’ s report when I was able to accompany her on the Congo mission with other Members, and I did indeed see that few peoples have shown such a clear desire to make democracy and to participate in it.
However, in order to believe in democracy, a people also need to believe in their state and their government, and this will not be possible with electoral or external support alone.
They also need a demand for the fight against corruption and to break the obscene paradox of living in misery in one of the richest countries in the world in terms of resources.

It must be said and condemned that part of the violence they suffer is imported or, at the very least, encouraged by unscrupulous multinationals from Europe or other continents or other States, which are capable of taking part in corruption, without caring about encouraging struggles for those resources in order to win the favour of those who run certain territories.

Therefore, Baroness Ashton, in your absolute competence, I would say that greater demands for traceability and transparency from European multinationals and others from other continents that are collaborating there would be as useful for the development of that people as the aid that we can provide them at an electoral and technical level.
Mr President, I would like to thank the rapporteurs for the valuable work they have presented.
Ladies and gentlemen, we are now debating package 2, after having approved package 6, after almost all Heads of State and Government agreed two international treaties outside the normal order.
EU, after many other European rules have created more EU authorities for banking supervision, stock market supervision, insurance supervision and for the risks of the economic system.
a proliferation of texts and more texts, of reports and proposals, which address new criteria for economic assessment, new procedures and new processes.
We should not stop and apply what we have already approved.
In the face of the instability that Europe is experiencing, we need the very opposite: stability and legal certainty.
We do not need a mass of successive legal findings.
I do not know, ladies and gentlemen, whether I am dreaming.
The situation that the rescue implies is very worrying.
The Spanish Government and State are not telling the Spanish people the whole truth about the new debt they are taking on as public debt by guaranteeing private debt.
The Spanish Government has requested aid that it previously said it did not want, and is ridiculously presenting as a triumph, as one of my fellow Members warned during this debate.
There is no triumph, only failure with Bankia and others.

I repeat, there is no more politically correct policy for Europe than the one sponsored by the French Government, behind which we must close ranks in Parliament, because we will not get out of debt without harmonious growth for all and for the whole.

Supervision, as requested in both reports, of course, all the supervision that is needed, but also simultaneous, anti-speculative, essential growth, as I welcome in some of the measures in both reports and other reports as well.
Please, clarity and transparency, and also balance, as my colleague, Mr Sánchez Presedo, said earlier.
Madam President, since I entered this Parliament, I have never stopped denouncing female genital mutilation.
This silent and accepted crime that millions of women and girls suffer in the world: 70 million, I repeat, 70 million
This is an unbearable figure for anyone who stops to study the terrible consequences of these practices, which are disguised as psychological, health or religious reasons, all of which are false.

The truth is that the ablation, which is performed preferably on girls aged 14 and even one year old, can lead to death from haemorrhagic collapse and, at best, cause and bring about serious psychological after-effects due to the trauma.
Europe must fight with all its strength to eradicate this human rights violation, which violates the right to equal opportunities and endangers health.
Ladies and gentlemen, this is abuse, cruelty and torture of girls, and this denigrating treatment also exists in Europe.
To this end, we must activate all the prevention mechanisms available to our legal system, and I encourage our judges to act to prevent genital mutilation.
of girls in Europe, punishing those who carry them out, so that the law in Europe speaks with one voice when combating mutilation on our soil.
Finally, I call on civil society and NGOs to become actively involved in education and prevention campaigns.
There is still a long way to go, but there are many of us who are not going to stop, because we owe it to the 70 million mutilated women.
Mr President, Commissioner, I am fully aware that one of the reasons why we adopted the dairy package was precisely to adapt the sector towards a quota-free dairy market in 2015.

However, it is also true that the closer this date of liberalisation gets, the greater the uncertainty in the sector, due to the lack of a clear exit strategy, and the more reasons there are for the sector to ask us to exercise prudence and reflection before making our decision.

reasons such as the belief in maintaining vineyard planting rights or sugar quotas until at least 2020, reasons such as the uncertainty in the sector regarding the real effects of the dairy package and whether

we would have had time to carry out a serious assessment of them, and also because of reasons such as uncertainty about the direction of the common agricultural policy and, in particular, its budget, that is, the part of income that does not come from the markets and that affects our producers.

Therefore, what we are asking you, Commissioner, and I am passing this on to Mr Cioloş, is that we do not carry out a piecemeal dismantling of our protection systems without a serious and rigorous analysis, because the effects on our producers are irreversible.

postpone the end of the quota until 2015, until a date that gives security to the sector, and open an open and transparent debate on regulatory mechanisms, because the markets are still more unfair and the producers are increasingly helpless.
Mr President, many Members of this Parliament have heard the voice of the citizens, the voice of the street.
Commissioner, you said that this is an agreement that is a case of acceptance or rejection.
Members of this Parliament also have a responsibility for the yes or the no.
We are saying no because this agreement is clearly confusing.
Intellectual property rights and the rights of internet users are not clarified.
Internet users are concerned about intellectual property rights.
Yes.
Yes, it is the right solution.
No, it is not the solution, because there are two issues to be addressed. This is not a legal issue, but two political issues: one, the determination of the fight against counterfeiting and piracy
piracy and another, a new legislative framework for the digital environment, which we in the Socialist Group have repeatedly asked for and are asking for again.
This is your chance.
Do not turn your back on society, because tomorrow it will turn its back on you and you will have no way out.
Sometimes it is necessary to increase the chaos in order to improve things.
The sense of powerlessness was so great that Europe had to react, and it has done so for the benefit of all, because the big winner of the last summit has been the euro.
With this victory, hope has returned that Europe will emerge from its paralysis and move from reflection to action.
Ladies and gentlemen, at this summit, both short-term measures to guarantee financial stability and long-term measures to resolve the Union ’ s shortcomings will be necessary.
In this case, the immediate was essential in order to continue working in favour of the important.
The imperative result is to send a message that the euro is a common, irrevocable and irreversible project.

Ladies and gentlemen, we welcome decisions as important as authorising the European Stability Mechanism to recapitalise banks directly, which will break the vicious circle between bank debt and sovereign debt, a thesis always defended, by the way, by the current Spanish Government.
Moreover, it will be used to intervene in markets in support of the public debt of countries that comply with their commitments on public deficits and reforms.
These were necessary decisions to guarantee financial stability, which must be the European Union ’ s top priority in the short term.
Ladies and gentlemen, this is not the time for certain countries to resort to nationalist maxims and put obstacles in the way.
As I have said, it is time to bet on Europe.
Ladies and gentlemen, Spain is adopting and implementing in record time the most ambitious reform agenda of the Spanish democracy, which includes a profound labour reform.
It is also carrying out a financial reform based on total transparency, and all this is demonstrating its commitment to fiscal consolidation and to meeting its deficit targets.

We also welcome the decision to launch a process for greater economic integration through four blocks: a banking union, a fiscal union, a common economic policy framework and strengthening democratic legitimacy.
Mr President, the Community, first and then the European Union, has always managed not only to survive crises, but to emerge from them stronger.
I am pleased that we have once again achieved this.
Ladies and gentlemen, we have all been focusing on the banks, which we have heard described time and again as the lifeblood of society.
This image is probably accurate, but let us bear in mind that every body is supported by a spine and joints.
Well, the backbone and joints of that social body we call Europe are precisely its transport networks and its energy infrastructures.
As has been said here in the Chamber this afternoon, it is essential to mobilise private funding and, where necessary, public funding.
Moreover, it is an opportunity to promote the issue of private debt guaranteed by European institutions, specifically aimed at these purposes and also open to any investor.
Congratulations to the rapporteurs on their work and also to the Commission for its clear support for these projects.

Mr President, Commissioner, Minister, I greet the new Presidency, I am a veteran activist of the now defunct Non-Aligned Movement, which your country led, and I very much desire the territorial integrity of Cyprus in the face of military occupations.

Ladies and gentlemen, we should have taken the opportunity to carry out a broader reform of the Court, perhaps, but the Commission and my fellow rapporteur have very positive intentions, and they should be supported unanimously, as they were in our committee.
The speed and good running of the Court are essential, and I have my doubts about this with the new, delayed patent jurisdiction.
Congratulations to the rapporteur. Go ahead with the reform of the Court.

Mr President, Commissioner Reding, my country has experienced too many setbacks since the Popular Party came to power: repeated and continuous attempts to roll back on established rights, such as silencing those who dissent, including consumer organisations.
The threat to AFACWA, stepping on Articles 16 and 17 of the Spanish Constitution, among others, by the Minister of the Minister of Mrs Mato, is a dangerous attack, not

not only freedom of expression and opinion, but also the right to a high level of consumer protection, which are basic principles of democracy and are enshrined in the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union.
I therefore ask the Rajoy Government to correct the wrong of having banned FACUA, and I call on the Commission to guarantee the protection of
the rights of Spanish consumers and act against the reduction in the rights and functions of consumer associations that the current Spanish Government is trying to impose.
Madam President, Commissioner, first of all, I would like to highlight the excellent work done by Mrs McAvan, the rapporteur, and also the other shadow rapporteurs, in reaching this
agreement at first reading, which was very important, as the legislation we adopted in 2010 was coming into force and needed to be revised.
Not because it was bad legislation – the legislation was very good – but as has been said, the recent events that we are all aware of occurred and showed that there was a certain
a loophole that needed to be closed to prevent it from happening again, and that specific points in the surveillance and safety of medicines once they are on the market needed to be strengthened.

Therefore, from now on, as has been said, holders of a marketing authorisation for a medicinal product who do not renew, withdraw or request the withdrawal or suspension of the authorisation will have
obligation to declare the real reasons and the motives that have led to this withdrawal or suspension of the authorisation of a medicinal product.
This is a great achievement in terms of transparency and will prevent the holder of a marketing authorisation from trying to hide the real reasons for his decision.
Moreover, in the event that any of these actions are carried out in a third country, all the Member States concerned where this medicinal product is marketed must be informed.
All this will improve transparency and safety without overburdening the European Medicines Agency and the national agencies.

The second important amendment that has been achieved is that all authorised products that are subject to post-authorisation safety studies are automatically included on a list of products subject to this follow-up.
Finally, we cannot forget that pharmacovigilance plays a fundamental role and, therefore, this plenary must adopt this proposal.
Mr President, the agreement reached by Parliament and the Council reflects what the States involved approved unanimously at the WTO.
These seas are in a worrying state in terms of acidification.
Moreover, their capacity for regeneration is very limited.
The fuels required will be more expensive and therefore financial aid is expected for entrepreneurs, which is very reasonable.
However, some of our colleagues have tried to apply the requirements for new fuels to all the seas of the European Union.

We would find ourselves in the paradox of wanting to solve an environmental problem in some countries in the Union by creating a major economic problem in the Mediterranean, which does not have that problem, but which has other environmental problems whose costs are not intended to be imposed on the rest of the countries.
The treaty is very clear.
Sensitive areas must be protected.
But not all areas are equally sensitive.

Mr President, I would like to thank once again all those who have made it possible for this directive to become a reality: the secretariat, the Commissioner, the rapporteur, all those whom I have forgotten and all the colleagues who have been with us.

I want to say to the victims, to all of them, absolutely all of them, without distinctions, that for us, they and their families have a face, they have a name, they are real, and precisely in their name,

we will work to ensure that this directive is not just left on paper, but that, with the commitment and help of Commissioner Reding, which I have absolutely no doubt about, it is implemented quickly and effectively, which is what we all expect and what all the
citizen.

I believe that there are different groups of victims who would have liked and still want their own directive, but I will tell them that the directive we will vote on tomorrow does not forget any victims and therefore does not forget them either.
I am sure that this is an important first step, which will be followed by many other steps that will open those doors to justice that have been closed for too long for victims.
When people ask me, as they do in my country, what I am doing in Europe, I will be able to say with great pride that I am legislating in favour of victims.
And that work, personally, deeply honors me and gives me a lot of strength to keep working.

I am sure that, with the support and commitment not only of those of us who work here in Europe, but also of our countries, we will succeed, together with you, in taking the victims out of that unjust oblivion.

Mr President, Mr Turmes has done an admirable job, because to get a regulation like this through, you need the courage of this rapporteur, who has known how to overcome the many obstacles skilfully put in his way.
It was worth it, because efficiency in the use of energy and its saving are requirements of any energy policy worthy of the name.

We should congratulate ourselves on important measures such as the requirement to refurbish public buildings, the imposition of a cumulative energy saving percentage on energy companies, and mandatory audits for large private companies.
However, the work of Mr Turmes and the other rapporteurs will be of no use if it is not accompanied by determined action from the Member States.
This action must in turn be accompanied by a firm commitment to these objectives on the part of the regions, municipalities and European citizens.
I would like to highlight the participation of the latter.
At the moment, what is evident is a great deal of frivolity among the authorities and among citizens.
Citizens. We do not see, ladies and gentlemen, in the harsh European winter, people sitting in heated outdoor cafes.
Mr Turmes, we must spare no effort in saving energy.
We should congratulate the rapporteur, Ms García Pérez, on the work she has done and congratulate ourselves on the agreement reached between Parliament and the Council of Ministers.
It is a pity that the Council is not with us this evening.
The new quality scheme for agricultural products simplifies the system and streamlines the authorisation procedures currently in place, and that is good news.

It is also a good achievement that the obligation to use the EU logo for protected designations of origin and protected geographical indications has been included, because this improves the visibility of European production.

I also think it is worth highlighting the fact that the provisions for protecting quality labels in bilateral agreements with third countries are being strengthened and that a legal basis is being created for
defend the EU logo in third countries, because I sincerely believe that protecting European products in third countries must be an objective that we cannot give up on.

It is a pity that, during the negotiations, this package has been somewhat weakened, and it is a shame, I say again, that the Council is not here to hear it, because the original quality package that was proposed
The Commission has somewhat forgotten commercial standards and the management of supply by quality designations, leaving the agreement somewhat empty.
I think we should criticise the rigidity shown by the Council on these two issues, in refusing to listen to Parliament ’ s positions, which are reasonable.
The management of supply by the designations of origin should have been defended, and has been defended, of course, in the WTO.

Mr President, the trade agreement with Central America, Colombia and Peru is an important agreement and the safeguard clause, what we are trying to do with the amendments we have tabled, is to make it easy, flexible and to provide guarantees.

We have tabled amendments to protect European production, in accordance with the Treaties, and we are establishing safety mechanisms for both European production and production in the countries where the agreement is established.
The outermost regions need to be protected because they do not have diversified crops, because of the economic factor, because it affects a large number of
jobs, more than 10 000 direct jobs and 35 000 indirect jobs in Spain, in a population that has been very hard hit by unemployment and, due to its specificity,
For example, the banana from the Canary Islands has a great specificity in agriculture, culture and gastronomy.

However, it is also necessary in the regions where we are going to establish the agreement, in Central America, Colombia and Peru, because trade is essential for development, and development means workers' rights, sustainability and benefits for the producer.

Mr President, the rapporteur has done a very good job, but I would like to take this opportunity to say here, in this context, that our prose is very confusing, as anyone who approaches European legislation can see.
We forget what Pufendorf already taught, that general laws should be few and their meaning must be correctly understood.
For this reason, those who issue them must be as clear as possible.
Montesquieu always stressed that legal language must be precise and concise.

In other words, we must avoid esoteric verbiage, full of neologisms and unjustified linguistic borrowings, because it creates a malignant network where traps are hidden and arbitrariness is established.
We deal with very complex issues in Parliament, but it is our duty to make them understandable and accessible to the citizens.
It is common for even legal professionals, judges and lawyers, to despair at our texts.
Ladies and gentlemen, good prose is the courtesy of the legislator and a way of showing respect for the citizen.
Mr President, Mr Posselt has just spent the little time he had insulting and denigrating from a position of the most complete ignorance.
Do you know the Spanish Constitution?

What the Vice-President, Mr Vidal-Quadras, said is nothing more or less than applying what is stated in the Spanish Constitution. Everything else is singular personal opinion, which is completely irrelevant and which I can respect but which I despise.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, there is an old principle that has passed from the legal sphere to the political sphere, and that is pacta sunt servanda, in other words, agreements are there to be honoured.
By this I mean the important measures adopted by the European Council in June to solve the crisis affecting us.

Since the adoption of these agreements, market pressure has eased and they have welcomed decisions that could break the vicious circle between banks and sovereign debt, as well as plans to move towards a genuine economic and monetary union and the commitment to defend the euro irreversibly.
The European Council held last week contains, ladies and gentlemen, undeniable positive aspects. The conclusions on progress towards a banking union are satisfactory.

The agreement reached by the 27 to launch the banking supervisor is also positive. It is finally appropriate that the launch should be entrusted to the Economic and Financial Affairs Council (ECOFIN).
But will you allow me to make a critical assessment of the biased interpretations that have unilaterally been made of it?

Ladies and gentlemen, it is not acceptable to make particular interpretations that could distort the mandate given to the Economic and Financial Affairs Council ministers, nor do we welcome issues that were not even discussed being reported as agreements.
We urgently need to move towards a single supervisory mechanism, which must cover all financial entities.
A common deposit guarantee scheme and a crisis resolution mechanism are also needed.
The survival of the euro is linked to the urgent creation of an effective banking union.
Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen, the advantages of the internal market are unquestionable: increased competition, greater supply of products and services,
services, the creation of new jobs, more affordable prices for consumers and greater protection – although not enough, and we cannot afford to be complacent.
However, cultural, linguistic, protectionist and bureaucratic barriers prevent citizens and companies from fully benefiting from the single market.
A year and a half ago, we held the presentation of the twelve priorities for stimulating growth and strengthening citizens ’ confidence in the internal market.
Unfortunately, an agreement has still not been reached on most of the proposals, as the Commission acknowledged in its communication last week.

Ladies and gentlemen, Commissioner, the urgency of the crisis requires us to be ambitious, to go much further and to reach agreements as soon as possible in order to promote growth, employment and confidence among citizens and businesses in the single market.
Mr President, I too would like to congratulate the rapporteur and the rest of the colleagues who have worked very well on this reform.

The financial crisis has increased due to the existence of complex and strange negotiable instruments linked to derivatives and over-the-counter transactions, all with the aim of achieving high returns at great speed.
Financial transactions need to be brought into a transparent market in order to guarantee good price formation and competition among negotiable products.
Above all, the information provided must be comprehensible to investors, who must have full knowledge in order to freely consent to the risks they are taking.
Transparency is useless as a bright flash of light if the confusion in the configuration of the negotiable products blinds citizens' understanding.
I like Mrs Thyssen's report because I believe that anything that strengthens Parliament's role and authority in the political debate is going in the right direction.
Too often, we hear accusations against the European institutions for their lack of democratic quality.
This is a statement made in passing, and this is not the occasion to enter into that debate.
Yes, to stress that this Parliament has the same legitimacy as any national parliament and is probably even governed by more transparent rules and allows its Members greater freedom than some.
Therefore, all economic measures must pass through the filter that this Parliament represents before final approval and afterwards when examining their implementation and results.
The group of experts designated to check in the Member States compliance with the conditions imposed by the Community authorities and the representatives of the
stability mechanism, must appear before this House so that we Members can have comprehensive and first-hand information.
Mr President, 5.3% of the working population work in the automotive sector.
The figures speak for themselves.
The fall in sales in the European market, together with business strategy, means that, in general terms, companies are outsourcing and restructuring.
There is a crisis of overcapacity affecting ten assembly plants and potentially 100 000 workers.

Unfortunately, there are few cases of restructuring with a long-term vision. Generally, a short-term vision prevails, in which the main element is to gain competitiveness on the basis of labour adjustments.

I have to say that on the same day that Ford communicated the closure of the plant in Genk, it communicated to the Ford company in Valencia that its production was going to be transferred to Germany, in the same business communication.
We therefore believe that this is a pan-European problem, and that it is essential to create a European legal framework that anticipates change and underlines the importance of social dialogue.
Social dialogue is essential.
Mr President, today we are debating four reports that represent a revitalisation of our common security and defence policy.
It is a clear sign of the conviction that our security and defence have a clear European dimension, in contrast to the minority of sceptics who also have a presence in this Chamber.

The report by Mr Danjean, for which I would like to thank him for his spirit of consensus during its preparation, contains important contributions from the Group of the Progressive Alliance of Socialists and Democrats in the European Parliament.
among others, references to effective multilateralism, to the principle of the responsibility to protect or to the concept of human security.
We would like to see these aspects included in the possible revision of the European Defence Strategy.
We have also pointed out that a strong common security and defence policy is also in the interests of our transatlantic alliance.
I must highlight another challenge that is acquiring major dimensions for our global security.
that of cybersecurity and cyber defence, a report that we are also debating today and whose suggestions we hope will be included in the new European cybersecurity strategy.
In a context of severe economic and financial crisis, in which Member States are being forced to cut their defence budgets, it is precisely then that European defence projects are most necessary.
That is why it is more important than ever to strengthen the role of the European Defence Agency, which is currently being questioned.
I conclude by saying that we can relaunch European security and defence and do so with Mr Van Rompuy's project for the December 2013 Council.
Thank you very much to the four rapporteurs.

Mr President, ladies and gentlemen, once again, the defence of the general interests defended by the Commission and Parliament runs up against national selfishness, which is entrenched in the Council.
Having accepted the legitimacy of both positions in advance, it is inexcusable not to reach a balance.
The relevance of the forthcoming financial framework is unquestionable.
The Spanish delegation of the PPE and I, on its behalf, reiterate that we find the Presidency ’ s proposal unacceptable.
Cutting EUR 75 billion from the Commission's proposal, which is already inevitably scarce, is pointless and useless.
Not only that: the new direct cut to agricultural and cohesion policies means going beyond the red line that Spain indicated from the start of the work.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is no longer time to discuss a supposed dichotomy between austerity and growth.
Some of us are making proven efforts and sacrifices to correct the deficit.
However, Spain, like many other countries, needs to grow, increase economic activity and create jobs.
This budget gets us nowhere; rather, it is a missile to the waterline.
Ladies and gentlemen, it is not possible to achieve more Europe with many fewer resources, particularly if what there is is distributed unfairly.

If that is the case, Spain will oppose a financial framework for the European Union that clearly and plainly means the disappearance of growth prospects and, above all, any trace of solidarity, and that is precisely the direction that the European Council is taking.
Fortunately, this is not the path indicated by the Commission, which we must thank for its commitment to indicating a different route.

On this point, on the need for unity, on the need for joint efforts, I would like to thank Mr Barroso for making a strong statement in Cadiz the other day, interpreting the Treaties for a supposed secessionist within the Union.

The guardian of the Treaties made it unequivocally clear that if part of a Member State separates to become an independent state, it will be considered a third country and the Treaties will therefore no longer apply to it.

I hope, Mr Vice-President, in the absence of the President, that this can be taken on board and that this safe and consistent doctrine of the Commission can continue to live on for everyone ' s guarantee.
